metadata.source: Hand-curated (project original) — see grammar-curated/ for input files
metadata.authorship_statement: All grammar explanations are written in our own words based on general, well-known, non-copyrightable facts about Japanese grammar. We do not copy from Tae Kim's Guide to Japanese (CC-BY-NC-SA, license incompatible with our CC-BY-SA 4.0 output), Dictionary of Basic Japanese Grammar, Handbook of Japanese Grammar Patterns, or any other copyrighted source. Every entry is marked review_status=draft and awaits native-speaker review. See docs/contributing.md for the call to native-speaker reviewers.
metadata.attribution: Grammar dataset from the Japanese Language Data project (https://github.com/jkindrix/japanese-language-data), CC-BY-SA 4.0.

field_notes.id: Stable project-assigned slug-form identifier. Used for cross-references.
field_notes.pattern: The grammar pattern in Japanese notation (e.g., '～ください', 'AはBより形容詞').
field_notes.meaning_en: Concise English explanation of what the pattern means or does.
field_notes.meaning_detailed: Longer explanation of nuance, usage, and contrast with related patterns.
field_notes.formation: How to form this pattern, written compactly (e.g., 'Verb-て-form + ください').
field_notes.formation_notes: Additional notes on formation, irregular cases, and exceptions.
field_notes.formality: Register: very_formal / formal / neutral / casual / intimate / vulgar.
field_notes.examples: Example sentences. Phase 3 examples are original (written by the project author, source: original). A future patch may cross-reference with Tatoeba sentence IDs by text match.
field_notes.review_status: One of: draft (not reviewed), community_reviewed (checked by community contributor), native_speaker_reviewed (verified by a native Japanese speaker). ALL initial Phase 3 entries are draft.
field_notes.reviewer_notes: Log of review comments. Empty on draft entries.
field_notes.sources: References consulted (or in the case of Phase 3 initial entries, general background) when writing this entry. NOT a list of sources we copied — we did not copy. The source field is for transparency about provenance of the factual claims.

grammar_points.pattern: V plain non-past / noun + にあたって / にあたり
grammar_points.meaning_en: on the occasion of / at the time of (formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にあたって marks an important occasion, milestone, or starting point at which the main-clause action is taken. It is formal and literary, common in speeches, formal announcements, and ceremonial contexts — 'on the occasion of X, (we do Y)'. Distinct from 〜際に (on the occasion of, more neutral) and 〜にさきだって (prior to).
grammar_points.formation: V plain non-past + にあたって / にあたり; noun + にあたって / にあたり.
formation_notes: Formal / ceremonial register — typical of speeches and formal announcements.
Marks significant occasions, not routine ones.
The variant にあたり is the slightly more literary form.
Common: 新年にあたって, 卒業にあたって, 出発にあたって.

examples.japanese: 新年にあたって、ご挨拶申し上げます。
examples.english: On the occasion of the new year, I offer my greetings.

examples.japanese: 出発にあたり、準備を整えた。
examples.english: In preparation for departure, I made ready.

examples.japanese: 卒業にあたって、先生に感謝を伝えた。
examples.english: On the occasion of graduation, I conveyed my thanks to my teacher.

grammar_points.pattern: V plain / noun + に先立って / に先立ち
grammar_points.meaning_en: prior to / in advance of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に先立って marks an action taken BEFORE an important upcoming event — 'prior to X, Y'. It is formal and usually appears in business and official contexts where preparation is explicit. The Y clause is the preparatory action; the X clause is the anticipated main event.
grammar_points.formation: V plain non-past + に先立って / に先立ち; noun + に先立って / に先立ち.
formation_notes: Formal / business register — for preparatory actions before an important event.
にさきだち is a slightly more literary variant.
Common: 会議に先立って, 開会に先立って, 発表に先立って.
Preparatory emphasis — distinct from simple 〜の前に (neutral 'before').

examples.japanese: 会議に先立って、資料を配布した。
examples.english: Prior to the meeting, I distributed the materials.

examples.japanese: 発表に先立ち、リハーサルを行った。
examples.english: In advance of the announcement, we held a rehearsal.

examples.japanese: 出発に先立って、パスポートを確認した。
examples.english: Prior to departure, I checked my passport.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / V plain + をきっかけに(して)
grammar_points.meaning_en: triggered by / with X as the impetus
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜をきっかけに introduces a triggering event that led to a significant change or new action — 'triggered by X, Y began / happened'. Often used for life-changing moments: a trip, a book, a meeting that sparked a new direction. Less formal than 〜を契機に.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + をきっかけに(して); V plain / V-た + の + をきっかけに(して).
formation_notes: Emphasizes the triggering moment — often something small or incidental that led to something big.
Less formal than 〜を契機に (which is more literary).
Common for life changes: 日本旅行をきっかけに日本語を勉強し始めた.
Often paired with 'started to' or 'came to' verbs describing new directions.

examples.japanese: 日本旅行をきっかけに、日本語を勉強し始めた。
examples.english: A trip to Japan triggered my starting to study Japanese.

examples.japanese: その本を読んだのをきっかけに、考え方が変わった。
examples.english: Reading that book triggered a change in my way of thinking.

examples.japanese: 病気をきっかけに、健康に気をつけるようになった。
examples.english: My illness triggered me to start caring about my health.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + を契機に / を契機として
grammar_points.meaning_en: taking X as the opportunity / prompted by
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を契機に is a more formal / literary variant of 〜をきっかけに, marking an opportunity or triggering event that led to a significant new direction. Common in news reports, business statements, and formal narratives. 契機 (けいき) literally means 'opportunity / turning point'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を契機に / を契機として.
formation_notes: Formal / written register — more literary than 〜をきっかけに.
Common in news and business: 事故を契機に、安全対策を強化した.
The longer form を契機として is equally acceptable, slightly more formal.
Often marks a watershed moment in company / government decisions.

examples.japanese: 事故を契機に、安全対策を強化した。
examples.english: Prompted by the accident, safety measures were strengthened.

examples.japanese: 留学を契機として、彼の人生は大きく変わった。
examples.english: Taking studying abroad as the turning point, his life changed greatly.

examples.japanese: 退職を契機に、趣味に時間を使うようになった。
examples.english: Prompted by retirement, he began spending time on hobbies.

grammar_points.meaning_en: due to / because of / per
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜につき has two related senses: (1) 'due to / because of' — giving a formal reason, common in signage ('closed due to renovation'); (2) 'per / for each' — a distributive sense in pricing or allocation ('$10 per person'). Strongly formal, common in business notices, signage, and official communications.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + につき (for both senses, disambiguated by context).
formation_notes: Reason sense: formal 'because of', often on signs: 工事中につき、通行禁止 ('closed due to construction').
Distributive sense: 一人につき1000円 ('1000 yen per person').
Formal / written register — avoid in casual speech.
Both senses share the same form — context disambiguates.

examples.japanese: 本日は定休日につき、閉店いたします。
examples.english: As today is our regular closing day, we are closed.

examples.japanese: お一人につき、1000円です。
examples.english: It's 1000 yen per person.

examples.japanese: 改装中につき、通路が狭くなっています。
examples.english: Due to renovations, the passageway has been narrowed.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + (が)ゆえに / 故に
grammar_points.meaning_en: because / therefore (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ゆえに is a literary / archaic causal marker meaning 'because X, therefore Y'. It is used in essays, formal writing, legal / philosophical texts, and classical styles. Modern speech rarely uses it, preferring から or ので. The standalone sentence-initial ゆえに can mean 'therefore'.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + (が)ゆえに; na-adj + (な / である) + (が)ゆえに; noun + の + ゆえに.
formation_notes: Literary / archaic register — rarely used in modern speech.
Common in legal, philosophical, and academic writing.
Can stand alone as 'therefore': ゆえに、結論は〜.
The optional が adds emphasis: 若きがゆえに ('precisely because he is young').

examples.japanese: 我思う、ゆえに我あり。
examples.english: I think, therefore I am.

examples.japanese: 若さゆえに、失敗することもある。
examples.english: Because of youth, one sometimes fails.

examples.japanese: 彼は正直であるがゆえに、損をすることも多い。
examples.english: Precisely because he is honest, he often loses out.

grammar_points.meaning_en: from the fact that / based on the fact
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ことから presents a fact or evidence as the basis for a conclusion, explanation, or designation — 'from the fact that X, (Y follows)'. Often used to explain the origin of a name, reputation, or conclusion drawn from observed evidence. Formal / written register.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ことから; na-adj + な / である + ことから; noun + である + ことから.
formation_notes: Evidence-based conclusion — X is the factual basis for Y.
Common in etymological / naming explanations: 桜の木が多いことから、この場所は'桜町'と呼ばれている.
Formal register — more common in written Japanese than casual speech.
Distinct from 〜から (simple causal) — ことから emphasizes factual grounding.

examples.japanese: 桜の木が多いことから、この町は'桜町'と呼ばれている。
examples.english: From the fact that it has many cherry trees, this town is called 'Sakura Town'.

examples.japanese: 足跡が残っていることから、犯人は2人だと分かった。
examples.english: From the fact that footprints remained, we knew there were two culprits.

examples.japanese: 彼の顔が赤いことから、お酒を飲んだことが分かる。
examples.english: From his red face, you can tell he's been drinking.

grammar_points.pattern: V plain / i-adj / na-adj な / noun の + あまり
grammar_points.meaning_en: out of excess / from too much
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜あまり expresses an excessive degree of emotion, sensation, or state that led to the main-clause consequence — 'out of too much X, Y happened'. Often with emotional nouns (驚き, 嬉しさ, 悲しみ) or strong verbs. Formal / written register. The main clause is typically an intense or unusual reaction.
grammar_points.formation: V plain + あまり; i-adj + さ + の + あまり; na-adj + な + あまり / noun (emotion) + の + あまり.
formation_notes: Expresses an excess of emotion / sensation leading to an unusual result.
Common with nominalized emotions: 驚きのあまり, 嬉しさのあまり, 悲しみのあまり.
The main clause often describes tears, fainting, or other intense reactions.
Formal register — rare in casual speech.

examples.japanese: 驚きのあまり、言葉を失った。
examples.english: Out of sheer surprise, I was lost for words.

examples.japanese: 悲しみのあまり、泣き出した。
examples.english: Out of too much sadness, I burst into tears.

examples.japanese: 働きすぎたあまり、体を壊した。
examples.english: From working too much, I ruined my health.

grammar_points.meaning_en: precisely because / it is only because
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ばこそ emphasizes the X clause as the sole or essential reason for Y — 'precisely because X, Y'. Formal / literary. Often used in sincere, heartfelt reasoning where the speaker wants to make it clear that Y would not be true without X. Compare with the more common 〜からこそ (see separate entry).
grammar_points.formation: V ば-form + こそ; i-adj 〜ければ + こそ; na-adj / noun + であれば + こそ.
formation_notes: Literary / formal — more emphatic than 〜からこそ.
Common in sincere / emotional assertions: 君を思えばこそ、こう言うのだ ('it's precisely because I care about you that I say this').
Close in function to 〜からこそ (more common) — differences are stylistic.
Distinct from 〜ば (simple conditional): ばこそ is not conditional but causal-emphatic.

examples.japanese: 君を愛していればこそ、こう言うのだ。
examples.english: It's precisely because I love you that I say this.

examples.japanese: 健康であればこそ、仕事ができる。
examples.english: It is only because one is healthy that one can work.

examples.japanese: 努力すればこそ、成功が得られる。
examples.english: Only by making effort can success be obtained.

grammar_points.meaning_en: precisely because / it is exactly because
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜からこそ emphasizes that the X clause is the essential and exclusive reason for Y — 'precisely because X, Y (and no other reason)'. More common than 〜ばこそ in modern speech, and slightly less literary. Often used in heartfelt reasoning, explanations, and endorsements.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + からこそ; na-adj / noun + だ + からこそ.
formation_notes: Common variant of 〜ばこそ — より neutral register.
Emphasizes that X is the essential reason, ruling out other potential causes.
Common in personal / emotional explanations.
The pair こそ (precisely) + から (reason) reinforces each other.

examples.japanese: 大切な友達だからこそ、厳しいことも言う。
examples.english: It's precisely because you're a dear friend that I say harsh things too.

examples.japanese: 難しいからこそ、挑戦する価値がある。
examples.english: Precisely because it's difficult, it's worth the challenge.

examples.japanese: 努力したからこそ、成功した。
examples.english: It's precisely because I worked hard that I succeeded.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / V plain + に伴って / に伴い
grammar_points.meaning_en: accompanying / along with / as X progresses
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に伴って expresses that one change or event accompanies another — 'along with X, Y also (happens / changes)'. Similar to 〜につれて and 〜にしたがって, but slightly more formal and with a stronger 'accompaniment' feel. Common in descriptions of social, economic, and environmental changes.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に伴って / に伴い; V plain + の + に伴って.
formation_notes: Formal register — common in news, reports, and academic writing.
に伴い is the slightly more literary variant.
Compare with 〜につれて / 〜にしたがって — に伴って has a slightly stronger 'accompaniment / concurrent change' feel.
The attributive form 〜に伴う + N is productive: 〜に伴う変化.

examples.japanese: 少子化に伴って、学校が減っている。
examples.english: Along with the declining birthrate, the number of schools is decreasing.

examples.japanese: 経済成長に伴い、都市化が進んだ。
examples.english: Along with economic growth, urbanization progressed.

examples.japanese: 技術の進歩に伴って、生活が便利になった。
examples.english: With the progress of technology, life has become more convenient.

grammar_points.meaning_en: depending on / in response to / in accordance with
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に応じて expresses proportional or responsive adjustment — the main clause action or outcome varies 'depending on' or 'in accordance with' the X clause. Formal / neutral register. Common for customization, adjustment, and adaptive responses. The attributive 〜に応じた + noun is productive.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に応じて (adverbial); noun + に応じた + noun (attributive).
formation_notes: Proportional / responsive — Y varies with X.
Common in service / customization contexts: 能力に応じて, 状況に応じて, 需要に応じて.
Attributive form に応じた: 能力に応じた仕事 ('work suited to one's ability').
Related: 〜次第 (depending on — more variable), 〜によって (similar, slightly more general).

examples.japanese: 能力に応じて、仕事が与えられる。
examples.english: Work is given according to ability.

examples.japanese: 状況に応じて、対応を変える必要がある。
examples.english: We need to change our response depending on the situation.

examples.japanese: 需要に応じて、生産量を調整する。
examples.english: We adjust production volume in response to demand.

grammar_points.meaning_en: in response to / responding to (expectations, requests)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にこたえて expresses that the main-clause action responds to or meets an expectation, request, demand, or cheer from the X noun. Common with 期待 (expectation), 要望 (demand), 要求 (request), 声援 (cheer). Formal / neutral register, common in announcements and reports.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にこたえて; attributive 〜にこたえた + noun.
formation_notes: Typical nouns: 期待, 要望, 要求, 声援, 願い.
Formal / written register, common in news and reports.
Similar to 〜に応じて but with a stronger 'meeting expectations' flavor.
The verb is 応える (to respond / meet) — the compound is literally 'in response'.

examples.japanese: 観客の声援にこたえて、選手たちはベストを尽くした。
examples.english: Responding to the audience's cheers, the athletes gave their best.

examples.japanese: 期待にこたえて、彼は見事に優勝した。
examples.english: Meeting expectations, he brilliantly won the championship.

examples.japanese: お客様の要望にこたえて、新商品を開発した。
examples.english: Responding to customer requests, we developed a new product.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form / noun + にかかわらず
grammar_points.meaning_en: regardless of / irrespective of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にかかわらず expresses that the main clause holds regardless of variation in the X factor — 'regardless of X, Y'. Often used with pairs or ranges that don't matter ('regardless of age / gender / weather'). Formal / neutral register. Distinct from 〜にもかかわらず (despite, concessive — see separate entry).
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にかかわらず; plain form (pair of opposites) + にかかわらず (e.g., 行く行かないにかかわらず).
formation_notes: Variation doesn't affect outcome — 'whatever the X, Y holds'.
Common with pairs / ranges: 年齢にかかわらず, 性別にかかわらず, 天気にかかわらず.
Verb pairs: 行く行かないにかかわらず ('regardless of whether you go'), 好き嫌いにかかわらず.
Distinct from 〜にもかかわらず (concessive 'despite').

examples.japanese: 年齢にかかわらず、誰でも参加できます。
examples.english: Regardless of age, anyone can participate.

examples.japanese: 天気にかかわらず、試合は行われる。
examples.english: Regardless of the weather, the match will be held.

examples.japanese: 経験のあるなしにかかわらず、挑戦してみてください。
examples.english: Regardless of experience, please try.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + にもかかわらず
grammar_points.meaning_en: despite / in spite of / even though
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にもかかわらず is a formal concessive marker meaning 'despite X, Y'. More formal than のに or が, with no emotional connotation — purely contrastive. Common in written Japanese, news, and formal speech. The main clause presents an unexpected or counter-intuitive continuation.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + にもかかわらず; na-adj + である + にもかかわらず; noun + にもかかわらず.
formation_notes: Formal concessive — neutral tone, no emotional weight.
More formal than のに (which can carry frustration) and が (neutral but less emphatic).
Common in news: 悪天候にもかかわらず、多くの人が集まった.
Distinct from 〜にかかわらず (variation doesn't affect outcome).

examples.japanese: 悪天候にもかかわらず、試合は行われた。
examples.english: Despite the bad weather, the match was held.

examples.japanese: 努力したにもかかわらず、結果が出なかった。
examples.english: Despite making an effort, no result came.

examples.japanese: 忙しいにもかかわらず、手伝ってくれた。
examples.english: Despite being busy, he helped me.

grammar_points.meaning_en: regardless of / without regard to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を問わず expresses that a main-clause condition or rule applies regardless of variation in the X factor — 'regardless of X'. Very close in meaning to 〜にかかわらず, but often used with categorical oppositions or with a 'question-not' framing. Formal / written register, common in official announcements and inclusivity statements.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を問わず. Often with pairs: 男女を問わず, 年齢を問わず, 経験の有無を問わず.
formation_notes: Formal / official register — common in inclusivity statements and recruitment.
Literally 'without asking / questioning X' — the variation in X is not considered.
Common paired nouns: 男女, 国籍, 年齢, 経験.
Very similar to 〜にかかわらず; choice is usually stylistic.

examples.japanese: 男女を問わず、応募できます。
examples.english: Regardless of gender, you can apply.

examples.japanese: 国籍を問わず、みんな歓迎します。
examples.english: Regardless of nationality, all are welcome.

examples.japanese: 経験の有無を問わず、参加可能です。
examples.english: Regardless of prior experience, participation is possible.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + はともかく(として)
grammar_points.meaning_en: setting X aside / putting X aside / regardless of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜はともかく sets aside a topic or factor as not worth discussing right now, to focus on something else — 'setting X aside, let's talk about Y'. Common in practical discussions to dismiss a side issue and focus on the main point. The longer form はともかくとして adds mild emphasis.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + はともかく / はともかくとして.
formation_notes: Dismisses X as beside the point to focus on Y.
Common in practical or strategic discussions: 値段はともかく、品質が大事だ.
The longer form はともかくとして is slightly more formal.
Related: 〜はさておき (even more formal / literary).

examples.japanese: 値段はともかく、品質を確認しましょう。
examples.english: Setting price aside, let's check the quality.

examples.japanese: 結果はともかく、最善を尽くすことが大事だ。
examples.english: Regardless of the result, doing one's best is what matters.

examples.japanese: 冗談はともかくとして、話を戻しましょう。
examples.english: Joking aside, let's get back to the topic.

grammar_points.meaning_en: setting X aside (formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜はさておき is a more formal variant of 〜はともかく, used to set a topic aside and move to another. Common in written Japanese, essays, and formal speech. The verb さておく literally means 'to set aside / put off', and the compound is transparent in meaning.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + はさておき.
formation_notes: Formal / literary variant of 〜はともかく.
Common in written Japanese and formal speech — less common in casual conversation.
The verb さておく ('set aside') shows through the compound.
Often used to redirect conversation away from a distraction.

examples.japanese: 冗談はさておき、真面目な話をしましょう。
examples.english: Setting jokes aside, let's have a serious conversation.

examples.japanese: それはさておき、次の議題に移ります。
examples.english: Setting that aside, let's move to the next topic.

examples.japanese: 詳細はさておき、概要を説明します。
examples.english: Setting details aside, I'll explain the outline.

grammar_points.meaning_en: of all (things/people/times) / precisely when
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に限って has two related senses: (1) 'of all X' — emphasizing that X is the exception or the one that (unexpectedly) did Y; (2) 'on this specific occasion' — often with a negative nuance ('of all times, today'). Common in complaint or surprise contexts: 'why did it have to happen today of all days?'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に限って + (typically negative or surprising main clause).
formation_notes: Contrastive / exceptional — marks X as the unexpected instance.
Often in complaints: 急いでいるときに限って、電車が遅れる ('of all times, the train is delayed when I'm in a hurry').
Also confidence: うちの子に限って、そんなことはしません ('not my child — he would never do that').
Distinct from 〜限り (scope / limit).

examples.japanese: 急いでいる時に限って、電車が遅れる。
examples.english: Of all times, the train gets delayed when I'm in a hurry.

examples.japanese: うちの子に限って、嘘はつかない。
examples.english: Not my child — he would never lie.

examples.japanese: 今日に限って、傘を忘れた。
examples.english: Of all days, today I forgot my umbrella.

grammar_points.meaning_en: not limited to / not only X (but also)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に限らず expresses that the main-clause statement applies not only to X but also to other, related cases — 'not limited to X, Y also'. Similar in function to 〜だけでなく / 〜ばかりでなく but with a scope / domain flavor rather than simple addition. Common in formal descriptions.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に限らず.
formation_notes: Scope expansion — the statement applies beyond X.
Often paired with も in the main clause: 日本に限らず、世界中で (... 'not only Japan but around the world').
More formal than 〜だけでなく, roughly equivalent in meaning.
Distinct from 〜に限って (exceptional / specific occasion).

examples.japanese: 日本に限らず、世界中でこの問題がある。
examples.english: Not only in Japan — this problem exists around the world.

examples.japanese: 子供に限らず、大人もこの本を楽しめる。
examples.english: Not limited to children, adults can also enjoy this book.

examples.japanese: 女性に限らず、男性も料理を楽しむ時代だ。
examples.english: This is an era when not only women but men also enjoy cooking.

grammar_points.pattern: V negative stem + ずにはいられない
grammar_points.meaning_en: can't help doing / can't stop oneself from
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ずにはいられない is a strong involuntary expression — 'can't help but do X'. The speaker feels compelled to act due to strong emotion or instinct. Formal register, common in literary and dramatic contexts. The casual equivalent is 〜ないではいられない.
grammar_points.formation: V ない-stem + ずにはいられない. Irregular: する → せずにはいられない.
formation_notes: Involuntary action — 'can't stop oneself'.
Irregular: する → せずにはいられない (not しずに).
More formal than 〜ないではいられない (see separate entry).
Common with emotional / spontaneous actions: 笑う, 泣く, 言う, 考える.

examples.japanese: あまりにもおかしくて、笑わずにはいられなかった。
examples.english: It was so funny, I couldn't help but laugh.

examples.japanese: 彼の優しさに、感動せずにはいられない。
examples.english: I can't help being moved by his kindness.

examples.japanese: この本を読むと、考えずにはいられない。
examples.english: Reading this book, I can't help but think.

grammar_points.pattern: V ない-form + ではいられない
grammar_points.meaning_en: can't help doing / can't stop oneself from (casual variant)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ないではいられない is the casual / neutral equivalent of 〜ずにはいられない — both express involuntary action compelled by emotion or instinct. Slightly less formal / literary. Common in everyday speech and informal writing.
grammar_points.formation: V ない-form + ではいられない.
formation_notes: Casual / neutral equivalent of 〜ずにはいられない.
Uses the everyday ない-form rather than the formal ずに.
Same meaning as the ずに variant — choice is register-driven.
Common with emotional reaction verbs.

examples.japanese: 面白くて笑わないではいられなかった。
examples.english: It was funny — I couldn't help but laugh.

examples.japanese: 彼女の話を聞いて泣かないではいられなかった。
examples.english: Hearing her story, I couldn't help but cry.

examples.japanese: 真実を知って、怒らないではいられない。
examples.english: Knowing the truth, I can't help being angry.

grammar_points.meaning_en: can't afford to be (doing X) / can't stay (in state X)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜てはいられない expresses that the speaker cannot afford to remain in a state or continue doing an action due to urgency or pressing concerns — 'I can't afford to be sitting around'. Often used when the speaker feels compelled to act because of an unfolding situation.
grammar_points.formation: V-て + はいられない (polite: いられません). Casual contraction: 〜ちゃいられない.
formation_notes: Urgency / pressing concern — the speaker can't stay inactive.
Casual contraction: 〜ちゃいられない (食べてはいられない → 食べちゃいられない).
Often appears with sitting, sleeping, watching: こうしてはいられない ('I can't just stand here').
Distinct from 〜ていられない (similar meaning, different form).

examples.japanese: こうしてはいられない。早く行こう。
examples.english: I can't just stay like this. Let's hurry.

examples.japanese: 試験が近いので、遊んではいられない。
examples.english: The exam is near, so I can't afford to play around.

examples.japanese: 彼の危険を思うと、寝てはいられない。
examples.english: Thinking of his danger, I can't just sleep.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + にしろ / にせよ
grammar_points.meaning_en: even if / whether or not (formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にしろ / 〜にせよ are formal literary variants of 〜にしても ('even if'). They can appear singly as a concessive, or in paired form (X にしろ Y にしろ — 'whether X or Y, either way'). More formal than 〜にしても and 〜ても. Common in formal / written Japanese.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause / noun + にしろ; plain-form clause / noun + にせよ. Paired: X にしろ Y にしろ.
formation_notes: Formal / literary variants of にしても.
Paired use (X にしろ Y にしろ) expresses 'whether X or Y, the point is...'.
せよ is slightly more literary than しろ.
Common in business and formal writing.

examples.japanese: 行くにしろ行かないにしろ、早く決めてください。
examples.english: Whether you go or not, please decide quickly.

examples.japanese: 失敗するにせよ、挑戦するべきだ。
examples.english: Even if you fail, you should try.

examples.japanese: どちらにしろ、結論は変わらない。
examples.english: Either way, the conclusion won't change.

grammar_points.meaning_en: even if / even assuming / even as
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜としても is a concessive marker meaning 'even if X', 'even assuming X'. It hypothetically accepts X and then states something that would hold regardless. More formal than 〜ても, with a slightly more analytical / considered feel. Can also mean 'even as (in the capacity of X)' when the preceding is a noun.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj / na-adj / noun + としても.
formation_notes: Hypothetical concession — 'even if we accept that X, Y'.
Noun sense: 教師としても、親としても ('even as a teacher / even as a parent').
More analytical than ても; common in explanatory contexts.
Distinct from としては (as for / from the perspective of).

examples.japanese: 失敗したとしても、経験にはなる。
examples.english: Even if I fail, it will become experience.

examples.japanese: 行くとしても、明日の朝だ。
examples.english: Even if I go, it will be tomorrow morning.

examples.japanese: 嘘だとしても、信じたい。
examples.english: Even if it's a lie, I want to believe it.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb volitional + と / ようと
grammar_points.meaning_en: regardless of / no matter (what / who / how)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: Verb volitional + と / ようと is a concessive construction — 'no matter what X', 'regardless of X'. Often paired with interrogatives (誰, 何, どこ) to express 'no matter who / what / where'. Formal / literary register. The paired form 〜うと〜まいと (see separate entry) covers 'whether or not'.
grammar_points.formation: V volitional (〜よう / 〜おう) + と. Often with interrogatives: 誰が〜ようと, 何を〜ようと.
formation_notes: Formal / literary concessive.
Common with interrogatives: 誰が何と言おうと ('no matter what anyone says').
Paired form 〜うと〜まいと covers 'whether or not' — separate entry.
Main clause expresses the speaker's resolve or an invariant fact.

examples.japanese: 誰が何と言おうと、私は諦めない。
examples.english: No matter what anyone says, I won't give up.

examples.japanese: 雨が降ろうと、試合は行われる。
examples.english: No matter if it rains, the match will be held.

examples.japanese: 何があろうと、私はあなたの味方だ。
examples.english: No matter what happens, I'm on your side.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb volitional + と + verb ます-stem + まい(と)
grammar_points.meaning_en: whether or not (one does) X
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜(よ)うと〜まいと is a paired concessive construction meaning 'whether X or not X, (the main clause holds)'. It combines the volitional + と (positive) with the negative まい + と. Formal / literary register, emphatic indifference to the positive/negative outcome of X. Common in assertions that hold no matter what.
grammar_points.formation: V volitional + と + V ます-stem / dict-form + まい(と). Example: 行こうと行くまいと, 食べようと食べまいと.
formation_notes: Paired 'whether X or not X' — covers both polarities.
まい is an archaic-literary negative volitional; the exact construction varies — some use V dict + まい, some use V ます-stem + まい.
For ichidan verbs: 食べる → 食べまい (or 食べるまい); for godan verbs: 行く → 行くまい; for する → すまい / しまい / するまい.
Formal / literary register — not common in casual speech.

examples.japanese: 行こうと行くまいと、あなたの自由だ。
examples.english: Whether you go or not, it's up to you.

examples.japanese: 彼が来ようと来まいと、会議は始める。
examples.english: Whether he comes or not, we'll start the meeting.

examples.japanese: 雨が降ろうと降るまいと、出発する。
examples.english: Rain or no rain, we're leaving.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb dict / ます-stem + まい
grammar_points.meaning_en: will not / probably not / intention not to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜まい is a literary / archaic negative volitional auxiliary with two senses: (1) 'won't X / will probably not X' (conjecture/negative prediction) — これ以上待つまい ('I won't wait any longer'); (2) 'intention not to X' — もう二度と行くまい ('I'll never go again'). Formal / literary register, rarely in casual speech.
grammar_points.formation: Godan / Ichidan V dict + まい (or 〜ます stem + まい for ichidan, varied). Irregular: する → すまい / しまい; くる → くまい / こまい.
formation_notes: Literary / archaic negative volitional — equivalent of modern ないだろう / ない.
Two senses: negative prediction ('won't') and negative intention ('will not').
For ichidan verbs: 食べまい or 食べるまい (both heard).
Rarely in casual speech; common in literary / historical contexts and fixed expressions.

examples.japanese: もう二度と彼とは会うまい。
examples.english: I'll never see him again.

examples.japanese: そんな簡単には負けるまい。
examples.english: I won't lose so easily.

examples.japanese: 彼は来まい。
examples.english: He probably won't come.

grammar_points.meaning_en: do X to the very end / thoroughly
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜抜く is an auxiliary meaning 'do X through to completion / to the very end'. Implies determination and often hardship — the action is completed despite difficulty. Common with endurance verbs: 走り抜く ('run through to the end'), 考え抜く ('think through thoroughly'), 生き抜く ('live through / survive').
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + 抜く (conjugates as godan: 抜きます, 抜いた, 抜いて).
formation_notes: Emphasizes completion despite difficulty or hardship.
Common with endurance / effort verbs: 走り抜く, 考え抜く, 生き抜く, 守り抜く.
Distinct from the independent verb 抜く ('pull out / extract').
Often implies admiration or respect for the perseverance.

examples.japanese: 最後まで走り抜いた。
examples.english: I ran through to the very end.

examples.japanese: 困難を乗り越えて、生き抜いた。
examples.english: Overcoming hardship, he survived through.

examples.japanese: じっくり考え抜いて決めた。
examples.english: I decided after thinking it through thoroughly.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb ます-stem + かねない
grammar_points.meaning_en: might / could (something bad)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜かねない expresses that something (usually negative) might happen or someone might do something — 'could do X', 'might do X'. Typically used for concerning possibilities, warnings, or cautionary predictions. Distinct from 〜かもしれない (neutral 'might') in that かねない carries a negative expectation.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + かねない (polite: かねません).
formation_notes: Negative prediction — typically expresses concern about an undesirable possibility.
Contrast with 〜かもしれない (neutral 'might').
Common in warnings: そんなことをすると、怪我をしかねない ('if you do that, you might get hurt').
The 〜かねる counterpart (see separate entry) means 'cannot / hard to do'.

examples.japanese: そんなに無理すると、病気になりかねない。
examples.english: If you push yourself that hard, you might get sick.

examples.japanese: 彼ならやりかねない。
examples.english: Knowing him, he might do it.

examples.japanese: 油断すると、大きな失敗をしかねない。
examples.english: If you're careless, you might make a big mistake.

grammar_points.meaning_en: cannot (quite) / hard to / difficult to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜かねる expresses that the speaker cannot (easily) do something — often for emotional, moral, or polite reasons rather than physical inability. Common in polite refusals and customer service: お答えしかねます ('I can't quite answer that'). More formal and softer than 〜できない. The opposite-polarity 〜かねない (see separate entry) means 'might'.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + かねる (polite: かねます).
formation_notes: Polite / softened inability — used for refusals and hedging.
Formal register — common in business and customer service.
Typical in polite refusals: お答えしかねます, ご要望にお応えしかねます.
Not for physical impossibility — use できない or V potential negative for that.

examples.japanese: その質問にはお答えしかねます。
examples.english: I cannot quite answer that question.

examples.japanese: ご要望には応じかねます。
examples.english: I'm afraid we cannot meet your request.

examples.japanese: 賛成しかねる意見だ。
examples.english: It's an opinion I can't quite agree with.

grammar_points.meaning_en: hard to do (emotionally / uncomfortably)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜づらい is a suffix meaning 'hard / uncomfortable to do X', with a focus on emotional or psychological difficulty rather than physical difficulty. Distinct from 〜にくい (physical difficulty) and 〜がたい (formal, often moral difficulty). Common for actions that cause discomfort, awkwardness, or emotional resistance.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + づらい. Functions as an i-adjective and conjugates regularly.
formation_notes: Emotional / psychological difficulty — 'awkward to do'.
Contrast with 〜にくい (physical difficulty) and 〜がたい (formal / moral).
Common with speech and social actions: 言いづらい ('hard to say'), 聞きづらい ('hard to ask').
Also common for discomfort: 歩きづらい ('hard to walk', e.g., on uneven ground).

examples.japanese: それは少し言いづらい話だ。
examples.english: That's a bit of an awkward topic to bring up.

examples.japanese: 彼に質問しづらい。
examples.english: It's hard to ask him questions.

examples.japanese: この靴は歩きづらい。
examples.english: These shoes are hard to walk in.

grammar_points.meaning_en: hard / difficult to (formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜がたい is a formal / literary suffix meaning 'hard to X' or 'difficult to X', often with a moral or intellectual nuance — things that are hard to accept, believe, or forgive. Distinct from 〜にくい (physical / practical difficulty) and 〜づらい (emotional discomfort). Functions as an i-adjective.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + がたい. Functions as an i-adjective.
formation_notes: Formal / literary — moral or intellectual difficulty.
Common fixed forms: 信じがたい ('hard to believe'), 想像しがたい ('hard to imagine'), 許しがたい ('hard to forgive').
Distinct from 〜にくい (physical difficulty) and 〜づらい (emotional discomfort).
Register: formal, common in written Japanese.

examples.japanese: それは信じがたい話だ。
examples.english: That's a hard-to-believe story.

examples.japanese: 許しがたい行為だ。
examples.english: It is an unforgivable act.

examples.japanese: 想像しがたい光景だった。
examples.english: It was a hard-to-imagine scene.

grammar_points.meaning_en: in order to / for the purpose of (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜べく is a literary purpose marker meaning 'in order to X'. It is derived from the classical form that also gives 〜べきだ ('should'). Common in formal writing, speeches, and official announcements. Main clause describes the action taken to achieve the purpose. Distinct from 〜ために (everyday purpose).
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + べく. Irregular: する → すべく (formal) or するべく.
formation_notes: Literary / formal purpose marker — replaces ために in formal writing.
The する form is usually すべく (literary contraction) rather than するべく.
Main clause expresses the action undertaken toward the goal.
Related: 〜べきだ (should), 〜べからず (must not — archaic).

examples.japanese: 夢を実現すべく、毎日努力している。
examples.english: In order to realize my dream, I work hard every day.

examples.japanese: 健康を取り戻すべく、運動を始めた。
examples.english: In order to regain my health, I started exercising.

examples.japanese: 事件を解決すべく、警察は調査を続けた。
examples.english: In order to solve the case, the police continued their investigation.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + はもちろん(のこと)
grammar_points.meaning_en: not to mention / of course / needless to say
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜はもちろん presents X as an expected or given case and the main clause adds an additional, sometimes more surprising, case — 'X goes without saying, and also Y'. Formal / neutral register, common in formal descriptions, speeches, and enumerations. The longer はもちろんのこと adds emphasis.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + はもちろん / はもちろんのこと.
formation_notes: X is the obvious case; Y is the additional / emphatic case.
Often paired with も in the Y clause: 日本人はもちろん、外国人も楽しめる.
The longer はもちろんのこと is slightly more formal.
Related: 〜はもとより (literary variant).

examples.japanese: 日本人はもちろん、外国人にも人気だ。
examples.english: It's popular not only with Japanese people but also with foreigners.

examples.japanese: 子供はもちろんのこと、大人も楽しめる。
examples.english: Needless to say children, but adults can also enjoy it.

examples.japanese: 漢字はもちろん、ひらがなも書けない。
examples.english: Not to mention kanji, he can't even write hiragana.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + を通じて / を通して
grammar_points.meaning_en: through / via / throughout (all of)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を通じて / 〜を通して has two related senses: (1) 'through / via' — by means of something (intermediary, medium, experience); (2) 'throughout / all through' — spanning the entire duration or extent of a period. The two variants を通じて and を通して are nearly interchangeable, with を通して slightly more neutral.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を通じて; noun + を通して. Attributive form: を通じての + noun.
formation_notes: Means / medium sense: 友達を通じて紹介してもらった ('was introduced through a friend').
Duration sense: 一年を通じて暖かい ('warm throughout the year').
を通じて is slightly more formal / literary; を通して is slightly more neutral.
Also appears as 〜を通じ / 〜を通し in formal writing.

examples.japanese: 友達を通じて彼に紹介された。
examples.english: I was introduced to him through a friend.

examples.japanese: 一年を通じて観光客が多い。
examples.english: There are many tourists throughout the year.

examples.japanese: この経験を通して、多くのことを学んだ。
examples.english: Through this experience, I learned many things.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + にわたって / にわたり / にわたる
grammar_points.meaning_en: over / spanning / across (a period or extent)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にわたって expresses that the main-clause action or state spans a period, area, or extent — 'over / throughout X'. Formal register. Common with time periods (3 years, decades), geographic ranges, and subject areas. The attributive form にわたる + noun is productive.
grammar_points.formation: Noun (time / area / domain) + にわたって / にわたり; noun + にわたる + noun (attributive).
formation_notes: Formal — for long periods or wide scope.
Common with time: 3年にわたって ('over 3 years'), 長年にわたり ('for many years').
Common with scope: 広範囲にわたる調査 ('a wide-ranging investigation').
Distinct from 〜の間 (more neutral 'during').

examples.japanese: 10年にわたって研究を続けた。
examples.english: I continued the research over 10 years.

examples.japanese: 長年にわたる努力が実を結んだ。
examples.english: Many years of effort bore fruit.

examples.japanese: 広範囲にわたり雨が降った。
examples.english: It rained over a wide area.

grammar_points.meaning_en: centered on / focusing on / mainly
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を中心に expresses that the main-clause activity or focus is centered on X — 'centered on X' / 'focusing on X'. Common in descriptions of themes, leadership, organization, and geographic focus. The attributive form 〜を中心とした + noun is productive in formal writing.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を中心に / を中心にして; attributive 〜を中心とした + noun.
formation_notes: Centered / focused on a core point or person.
Common with leadership / organization: 社長を中心に ('centered around the president').
Common with subject focus: 環境問題を中心に話し合う ('discuss centered on environmental issues').
The attributive form 〜を中心とした + noun: 〜を中心とした活動 ('activities centered on ~').

examples.japanese: 環境問題を中心に話し合った。
examples.english: We discussed, focusing on environmental issues.

examples.japanese: この地域は農業を中心に発展してきた。
examples.english: This region has developed centered on agriculture.

examples.japanese: 若者を中心に、この音楽が流行している。
examples.english: Centered on young people, this music is popular.

grammar_points.meaning_en: with (feeling) / putting (feeling) into
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を込めて expresses doing an action with a particular feeling or emotion 'put into' it — 'with love', 'with care', 'with gratitude'. Common with emotional nouns (愛, 感謝, 心, 思い, 願い). Often appears in dedications and heartfelt expressions.
grammar_points.formation: Noun (emotion) + を込めて.
formation_notes: Common emotional nouns: 愛, 感謝, 心, 思い, 願い, 情熱.
Often in dedications: 愛を込めて手紙を書いた ('wrote a letter with love').
Attributive form: 〜を込めた + noun.
Formal / sentimental register.

examples.japanese: 愛を込めて手紙を書いた。
examples.english: I wrote the letter with love.

examples.japanese: 感謝を込めてプレゼントを贈る。
examples.english: I give a present with gratitude.

examples.japanese: 心を込めて歌った。
examples.english: I sang with all my heart.

grammar_points.meaning_en: for (a) / considering X (surprising)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にしては expresses that the main-clause observation is surprising considering the X premise — 'for an X, (Y is unexpected)'. Often used when the main clause describes something that exceeds or falls short of expectations for someone in the X category. Similar to 〜わりに(は) but with a slightly more explicit 'given the category' framing.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にしては.
formation_notes: Category-based comparison — 'for a X, this is unexpected'.
Often with age, experience, role: 子供にしては, 初めてにしては, 新人にしては.
Similar to 〜わりに(は) — both express surprise given a standard.
にしては highlights the category; わりに is more about degree.

examples.japanese: 初めてにしては、上手に弾けた。
examples.english: For a first try, I played it well.

examples.japanese: 子供にしては、よく知っている。
examples.english: For a child, he knows a lot.

examples.japanese: 新人にしては、仕事ができる。
examples.english: For a newcomer, he's capable at his work.

grammar_points.meaning_en: at (the point of) / being (X), (emphatic)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にして has several related literary senses: (1) at a specific (often emphatic) point — '80歳にして' ('at 80'); (2) 'being X (simultaneously Y)' — a noun modifier combining two statuses. Formal / literary register, common in fixed expressions like 'once in a lifetime' and age-emphasizing contexts.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にして. Typically with age, position, or identity nouns.
formation_notes: Age emphasis: 80歳にして ('at 80 years old', emphasizing the age as notable).
Dual-identity sense: 医者にして作家 ('a doctor who is also a writer').
Literary / formal — rare in casual speech.
Often in fixed rhetorical phrases: 一瞬にして ('in an instant'), 今にして思えば ('now that I think about it').

examples.japanese: 彼は80歳にして、今も元気だ。
examples.english: At 80 years old, he is still energetic.

examples.japanese: 今にして思えば、あれが最後のチャンスだった。
examples.english: Now that I think about it, that was my last chance.

examples.japanese: 彼は医者にして作家でもある。
examples.english: He is a doctor and also a writer.

grammar_points.pattern: V-た form / noun + の + 末(に)
grammar_points.meaning_en: after (a long process) / at the end of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜末に marks the conclusion of a lengthy process, deliberation, or struggle — 'after much X, finally Y'. The main clause describes the outcome or decision that emerged from the drawn-out process. Common with verbs of thinking, struggling, discussing. Formal / written register.
grammar_points.formation: V-た + 末(に); noun + の + 末(に). Attributive: 〜末の + noun.
formation_notes: Implies a long or difficult process before the outcome — not just a simple 'after'.
Common with deliberation verbs: 悩んだ末, 考えた末, 話し合った末.
Attributive: 苦労の末の成功 ('success after hardship').
Distinct from 〜た後で (neutral 'after').

examples.japanese: 長い間悩んだ末、留学することにした。
examples.english: After agonizing for a long time, I decided to study abroad.

examples.japanese: 議論の末、結論が出た。
examples.english: After much discussion, a conclusion was reached.

examples.japanese: 苦労の末、成功をつかんだ。
examples.english: After many hardships, he finally grasped success.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + だけのことはある
grammar_points.meaning_en: (it's) as expected from / lives up to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜だけのことはある expresses that the main-clause praise or quality is fitting given a stated premise — 'being X, it's no surprise that Y' / 'Y lives up to the expectations of X'. Positive evaluative, very similar to 〜だけあって (N3) but in a slightly more emphatic, reflective tone.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + だけのことはある; na-adj + な + だけのことはある; noun + (である) + だけのことはある.
formation_notes: Positive evaluative — praise that 'fits' the premise.
Very close in meaning to 〜だけあって (N3); this form is more reflective / conclusive.
Often used after observing or experiencing the main-clause quality: 〜だけのことはあるね ('no wonder').
Common in reviews and compliments.

examples.japanese: 長年練習しただけのことはあって、演奏が素晴らしい。
examples.english: His practice of many years shows — the performance is splendid.

examples.japanese: 高いだけのことはある。本当に美味しい。
examples.english: It lives up to its high price — really delicious.

examples.japanese: プロだけのことはあるね。
examples.english: He really lives up to being a pro.

grammar_points.meaning_en: no need to even / not worth (doing)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜までもない expresses that an action is so unnecessary or obvious that it isn't even worth doing — 'no need to go so far as X'. Often used to dismiss a suggested action as overkill, or to introduce something obvious with 言うまでもない ('needless to say'). Formal / neutral register.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + までもない. Fixed phrase: 言うまでもない / 言うまでもなく.
formation_notes: Dismisses an action as excessive or unnecessary.
Very common fixed phrase: 言うまでもない ('it goes without saying').
Distinct from 〜まで (scope 'as far as') and 〜までに (deadline).
Often follows a minor problem where the suggested response would be overkill.

examples.japanese: 言うまでもなく、日本の首都は東京だ。
examples.english: Needless to say, Japan's capital is Tokyo.

examples.japanese: そんなことで病院に行くまでもない。
examples.english: There's no need to go so far as to visit a hospital for that.

examples.japanese: わざわざ行くまでもないよ。
examples.english: No need to go out of your way.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + というものだ
grammar_points.meaning_en: that's what X is / that's (the nature of) X
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜というものだ expresses a speaker's evaluative conclusion or universal statement about what something is or should be — 'that's what X is' / 'that's the way of things'. Often carries a moralizing or reflective tone, stating a general truth or principle. Distinct from 〜ものだ (general tendency / nostalgic 'used to') — というものだ is more evaluative.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + というものだ; noun + というものだ.
formation_notes: Evaluative / normative — 'that's the nature of it'.
Often preceded by a definition or statement the speaker is reframing.
Negative form 〜というものではない is covered as a separate entry.
Distinct from 〜ものだ (general truth / nostalgic) and 〜ということだ (hearsay 'I hear that').

examples.japanese: 困っている人を助けるのが友情というものだ。
examples.english: Helping a friend in need — that's what friendship is.

examples.japanese: それが現実というものだ。
examples.english: That's what reality is.

examples.japanese: 努力すれば報われる、というものではないかもしれない。
examples.english: Making an effort doesn't always guarantee a reward, perhaps.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + というものではない
grammar_points.meaning_en: it's not simply (a matter of) / it's not that
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜というものではない negates a simplistic claim, arguing that the situation is more nuanced than the stated X — 'it's not simply that X' / 'it doesn't just come down to X'. Common in discussions where the speaker is correcting an oversimplification.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + というものではない / というものでもない.
formation_notes: Corrects an oversimplification — insists on nuance.
Common in arguments and essays that resist easy conclusions.
Related to 〜というものだ (positive evaluative); this is the negated form.
〜というものでもない is a slightly softer variant.

examples.japanese: お金があれば幸せになれるというものではない。
examples.english: It's not simply that having money makes you happy.

examples.japanese: 勉強すれば必ず成功するというものではない。
examples.english: It's not just that studying guarantees success.

examples.japanese: 謝れば済むというものではない。
examples.english: It's not just a matter of apologizing to make it right.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + ものだから / もので
grammar_points.meaning_en: because / it's because (excusing / justifying)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ものだから gives a reason with an excuse-making or justifying flavor — 'it's because X (so please understand)'. Often used when the speaker is explaining why they did something unexpected or failed to meet an expectation. The shorter form 〜もので is slightly softer. Distinct from 〜んだから (more emphatic) and 〜から (neutral).
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ものだから; na-adj + な + ものだから; noun + な + ものだから.
formation_notes: Excuse-making — often used to explain tardiness or failures.
The short form 〜もので is softer / more polite.
More apologetic than plain から or のだから.
Common in polite apologies: 忙しいものですから…

examples.japanese: 急いでいたものだから、挨拶もせずに帰ってしまった。
examples.english: Because I was in a hurry, I left without even saying goodbye.

examples.japanese: 最近忙しいもので、連絡が遅くなりました。
examples.english: I've been busy lately, so my reply was delayed.

examples.japanese: 小さい子供がいるものですから、夜は外出できません。
examples.english: Since I have small children, I can't go out at night.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + やら + noun + やら
grammar_points.meaning_en: things like X and Y (disordered / overwhelming)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜やら〜やら is a non-exhaustive list construction similar to 〜とか〜とか, but with a flavor of disarray, overwhelm, or mess. Often used when describing a situation as chaotic or a mix of unpleasant items — 'what with X and Y'. Can also combine adjectives or verb plain forms. Formal / literary register compared to とか.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + やら + noun + やら; verb plain / i-adj + やら + verb / i-adj + やら.
formation_notes: Disordered / overwhelmed list — often describing a messy situation.
More formal / literary than とか.
Can also express uncertainty: 誰がやったのやら ('who knows who did it').
Often pairs things of different categories to emphasize disarray.

examples.japanese: 宿題やら掃除やらで忙しい。
examples.english: I'm busy with homework and cleaning and whatnot.

examples.japanese: 雨やら風やらで、外に出られなかった。
examples.english: What with the rain and the wind, I couldn't go out.

examples.japanese: 嬉しいやら恥ずかしいやら、複雑な気持ちだった。
examples.english: I felt happy and embarrassed and all mixed up.

grammar_points.meaning_en: judging from / starting with (as a representative example)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜からして has two related senses: (1) 'judging from X' — where X is given as a basis for the main-clause judgment; (2) 'starting with X (as even this basic example shows)' — emphasizing a minimal representative case. Common in evaluative statements: '(if even) X is like this, (imagine the rest)'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + からして.
formation_notes: Judging from the most minimal/basic example — often implies worse beyond.
Common: 見た目からして怪しい ('suspicious just from the look of it').
Distinct from 〜から (reason) and 〜からすると (standpoint).
Often at the start of criticism or evaluation chains.

examples.japanese: あの人は話し方からして失礼だ。
examples.english: That person is rude — just from the way they speak, you can tell.

examples.japanese: 見た目からして、すごく美味しそうだ。
examples.english: Just from the look, it seems very delicious.

examples.japanese: 名前からしてユニークだ。
examples.english: Starting with the name, it's unique.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + からすると / からすれば
grammar_points.meaning_en: from X's standpoint / judging from
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜からすると / 〜からすれば expresses that the main clause is judged from the perspective of X — 'from X's standpoint' or 'judging from X'. Similar to 〜にとって (from the perspective of) but emphasizes the act of judging or inferring. Common in analytical and evaluative contexts.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + からすると / からすれば.
formation_notes: Judging / inferring from a standpoint — often analytical.
からすると and からすれば are nearly interchangeable; すると is slightly more common in speech.
Similar but distinct from にとって (which is more about value / meaning TO someone).
Common in analytical / evaluative statements.

examples.japanese: 専門家からすると、この問題は簡単だ。
examples.english: From an expert's standpoint, this problem is easy.

examples.japanese: 彼の話し方からすれば、怒っているようだ。
examples.english: Judging from how he's speaking, he seems angry.

examples.japanese: 外国人からすると、日本語の漢字は難しい。
examples.english: From a foreigner's standpoint, Japanese kanji are difficult.

grammar_points.meaning_en: now that / since (with strong obligation)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜からには expresses a strong commitment based on a premise — 'now that X, one must Y'. The main clause typically expresses obligation, determination, or strong consequence arising from the X premise. Similar in function to 〜以上は but with slightly more emotional weight. Formal register.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + からには; na-adj / noun + である + からには.
formation_notes: Strong obligation from a premise — 'since this is the case, you must'.
Similar to 〜以上(は) but emotionally stronger.
Often used in declarations of commitment: 約束したからには、守ります ('now that I've promised, I'll keep it').
Formal / determined register.

examples.japanese: 約束したからには、必ず守ります。
examples.english: Now that I've promised, I will definitely keep it.

examples.japanese: 日本に来たからには、日本語をマスターしたい。
examples.english: Now that I've come to Japan, I want to master Japanese.

examples.japanese: やるからには、最後までやる。
examples.english: If I'm going to do it, I'll see it through.

grammar_points.meaning_en: not only X but also Y (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜のみならず is a literary 'not only' construction — the most formal variant of the 'not only' family (〜だけでなく, 〜ばかりでなく, 〜のみならず). Common in essays, formal speeches, and written Japanese. Often paired with も in the main clause.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + のみならず; na-adj + である + のみならず; noun + のみならず.
formation_notes: Most formal / literary of the 'not only' family.
Rare in casual speech — common in essays, news, and formal writing.
Often paired with も in the Y clause.
のみ is a literary 'only' particle; のみならず = 'not only'.

examples.japanese: 彼は頭がいいのみならず、性格もいい。
examples.english: Not only is he smart, but he also has a good personality.

examples.japanese: この問題は日本のみならず、世界中で議論されている。
examples.english: This issue is debated not only in Japan but around the world.

examples.japanese: 学生のみならず、大人にも役立つ本だ。
examples.english: This book is useful not only for students but also for adults.

grammar_points.meaning_en: although / that said / even so
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とはいえ is a formal concessive meaning 'although X' or 'that said, X'. It hedges a statement by noting that while X is true, the main clause presents a contrasting or tempering observation. Literary / formal register, common in essays and written Japanese.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + とはいえ; na-adj / noun (+ だ) + とはいえ.
formation_notes: Literary / formal concessive — 'that said'.
Common in essays, news, and balanced discussions.
Similar to 〜とは言うものの and 〜といっても.
The main clause often moderates or contrasts the X premise.

examples.japanese: もう春とはいえ、まだ寒い。
examples.english: Although it's already spring, it's still cold.

examples.japanese: 忙しいとはいえ、家族の時間は大切だ。
examples.english: Busy as I am, family time is important.

examples.japanese: 子供とはいえ、責任感がある。
examples.english: Although he's a child, he has a sense of responsibility.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + とはいうものの
grammar_points.meaning_en: that said / although one says
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とはいうものの is a literary concessive similar to 〜とはいえ — 'although one says X, (the reality is Y)'. Often used to qualify a general statement by pointing out exceptions or nuances. More drawn-out and reflective than 〜とはいえ. Common in written Japanese.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + とはいうものの; na-adj / noun + (だ) + とはいうものの.
formation_notes: Reflective / nuance-adding concessive.
More drawn-out than とはいえ — emphasizes the speaker's consideration.
Common in essays and opinion pieces.
The main clause qualifies or tempers the X statement.

examples.japanese: 春だとはいうものの、今朝は雪が降った。
examples.english: Although we say it's spring, snow fell this morning.

examples.japanese: お金が大事だとはいうものの、それがすべてではない。
examples.english: Although we say money is important, it's not everything.

examples.japanese: 便利だとはいうものの、問題もある。
examples.english: Although it's called convenient, it also has problems.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + にしてみれば / にしてみたら
grammar_points.meaning_en: from X's perspective / from X's point of view
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にしてみれば / 〜にしてみたら expresses the main-clause observation from the perspective of a specific person or group — 'from X's point of view, it seems Y'. More introspective / empathetic than 〜からすると, often used to convey what someone else might feel or think about a situation.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にしてみれば / にしてみたら.
formation_notes: Empathetic / introspective perspective — 'from their point of view'.
Similar to 〜からすると but with a stronger 'putting oneself in X's shoes' flavor.
Common in sympathizing with others: 子供にしてみれば、当然の反応だ ('from the child's perspective, it's a natural reaction').
Less formal than にとって but more perspective-oriented.

examples.japanese: 子供にしてみれば、当然の反応だ。
examples.english: From a child's perspective, it's a natural reaction.

examples.japanese: 私にしてみれば、そんなのは大した問題じゃない。
examples.english: From my perspective, that's not a big deal.

examples.japanese: 外国人にしてみたら、難しいかもしれない。
examples.english: From a foreigner's perspective, it may be difficult.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + からといって
grammar_points.meaning_en: just because / even though (one says)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜からといって rejects a simplistic causal inference — 'just because X (doesn't mean Y)'. The main clause often uses a negative or 'not necessarily' predicate to refute the naive inference. Very common in careful reasoning and avoiding overgeneralization.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + からといって (+ negative or hedged main clause).
formation_notes: Rejects a simple 'X therefore Y' inference — 'just because X, doesn't mean Y'.
Main clause usually hedged: とは限らない, わけではない.
Common in argumentation and careful analysis.
Distinct from 〜といって (similar form, different sense).

examples.japanese: お金があるからといって、幸せとは限らない。
examples.english: Just because you have money doesn't necessarily mean you're happy.

examples.japanese: 若いからといって、何でも許されるわけではない。
examples.english: Just because you're young doesn't mean everything is forgiven.

examples.japanese: 高いからといって、必ずしもいいとは限らない。
examples.english: Just because it's expensive doesn't mean it's necessarily good.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + の + ことだから
grammar_points.meaning_en: knowing X / given that it's X
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜のことだから expresses that the speaker is making a prediction based on knowledge of X's character or habits — 'knowing X, (they probably...)'. Often used for third-person predictions that the speaker considers highly likely given what they know about the subject. The X is typically a person or entity.
grammar_points.formation: Noun (person) + の + ことだから.
formation_notes: Personal-knowledge prediction — based on the speaker's familiarity with X.
Almost always with a subject the speaker knows well: ability, habits, personality.
Main clause is typically a prediction with だろう / でしょう / に違いない.
Not quite synonymous with 〜ことから (factual evidence) — 〜のことだから is about character.

examples.japanese: 時間に厳しい田中さんのことだから、きっと時間通りに来るだろう。
examples.english: Knowing Mr. Tanaka, who is strict about time, he'll surely come on time.

examples.japanese: 真面目な彼のことだから、ちゃんと宿題をしているはずだ。
examples.english: Knowing he's so diligent, he must be doing his homework properly.

examples.japanese: 忘れっぽい私のことだから、また鍵を忘れるかもしれない。
examples.english: Knowing forgetful me, I might forget my keys again.

grammar_points.pattern: V-て form + からというもの
grammar_points.meaning_en: ever since / since that time
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜てからというもの emphasizes a dramatic change or ongoing state that began from a specific past event — 'ever since X, Y has continued / changed'. More emphatic than 〜て以来, often used when the change is notable or the speaker is nostalgic / reflective. Formal register.
grammar_points.formation: V-て + からというもの + (main clause describing ongoing state).
formation_notes: Emphatic / reflective 'ever since' — stronger than 〜て以来.
Main clause typically describes a continuous or habitual change resulting from the X event.
Formal / literary register.
Often paired with nostalgic or transformative context: 結婚してからというもの、生活が変わった.

examples.japanese: 日本に来てからというもの、毎日が楽しい。
examples.english: Ever since I came to Japan, every day has been enjoyable.

examples.japanese: その本を読んでからというもの、考え方が大きく変わった。
examples.english: Ever since I read that book, my way of thinking has changed greatly.

examples.japanese: 子供が生まれてからというもの、自由な時間が全くない。
examples.english: Ever since my child was born, I have had no free time at all.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb ます-stem + つつある
grammar_points.meaning_en: in the process of / gradually becoming
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜つつある expresses that an action or change is currently in progress or gradually happening — 'is in the process of X'. Often used for large-scale, long-term, or gradual changes (social, environmental, personal). More formal than 〜ている (which is the everyday progressive). Common in reports and formal descriptions of trends.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + つつある.
formation_notes: Formal progressive — for gradual or ongoing change.
Common with change verbs: 変わる, 増える, 減る, 発展する, 進む.
Distinct from 〜ている (everyday progressive / state) — 〜つつある emphasizes slow progression.
Formal / written register, common in news and reports.

examples.japanese: 地球温暖化が進みつつある。
examples.english: Global warming is progressing.

examples.japanese: 日本の人口は減りつつある。
examples.english: Japan's population is gradually decreasing.

examples.japanese: 状況は改善しつつある。
examples.english: The situation is gradually improving.

grammar_points.meaning_en: at least X (is better than nothing) / could be worse
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜だけましだ expresses a minor consolation — 'at least X is the case, and that's better than worse alternatives'. Used when finding a silver lining in a negative situation: it's not great, but at least it's not as bad as it could be. The main clause often implicitly or explicitly compares to a worse hypothetical.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + だけましだ; na-adj + な + だけましだ; noun + (な) + だけましだ.
formation_notes: Minor consolation — silver lining in a negative situation.
Often with implicit comparison to something worse.
ましだ as a standalone is also common: まだましだ ('it's still better').
Conversational register; less common in formal writing.

examples.japanese: 雨だけど、雪じゃないだけましだ。
examples.english: It's raining, but at least it's not snowing.

examples.japanese: 給料は低いが、仕事があるだけましだ。
examples.english: My salary is low, but at least I have a job.

examples.japanese: 風邪は辛いが、熱がないだけましだ。
examples.english: Having a cold is rough, but at least I don't have a fever.

grammar_points.meaning_en: as soon as / the moment that (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜や否や is a literary / formal construction meaning 'as soon as X, Y'. The main clause event follows immediately and without interval. More dramatic and literary than 〜たとたん or 〜と同時に. Common in narratives and reports where the speaker wants to emphasize instantaneous cause and effect.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + や否や.
formation_notes: Literary / dramatic 'as soon as'.
The kanji 否 means 'no / deny' — etymologically 'whether or not'.
Common in news reports and narratives.
Related: 〜たとたん (N3), 〜か〜ないかのうちに (next entry).

examples.japanese: 彼は家に着くや否や、ベッドに倒れ込んだ。
examples.english: As soon as he got home, he collapsed into bed.

examples.japanese: ドアを開けるや否や、猫が飛び出した。
examples.english: The moment I opened the door, the cat jumped out.

examples.japanese: 始業のベルが鳴るや否や、生徒たちは席についた。
examples.english: The moment the opening bell rang, the students took their seats.

grammar_points.pattern: V dict + か + V ない + かのうちに
grammar_points.meaning_en: no sooner (had X) than Y / barely had one Xed when Y
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜か〜ないかのうちに expresses that the main-clause event happened almost simultaneously with or even before the X event completed — 'no sooner had X happened than Y'. The two events overlap so tightly that the boundary is ambiguous. Formal / literary.
grammar_points.formation: V dict + か + same V ない-form + かのうちに + main clause.
formation_notes: Near-simultaneous events — the main clause event is nearly overlapping.
More emphatic of simultaneity than 〜たとたん.
Formal / literary — rare in casual speech.
Related: 〜や否や (same idea, slightly more dramatic), 〜とたん (N3).

examples.japanese: 家に着くか着かないかのうちに、雨が降り出した。
examples.english: No sooner had I arrived home than it started to rain.

examples.japanese: ベルが鳴るか鳴らないかのうちに、生徒たちは教室を出た。
examples.english: No sooner had the bell rung than the students left the classroom.

examples.japanese: 目を閉じるか閉じないかのうちに、眠ってしまった。
examples.english: No sooner had I closed my eyes than I fell asleep.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb plain / V-た form + そばから
grammar_points.meaning_en: as soon as / immediately after (with repeated undoing)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜そばから expresses 'no sooner had X happened than Y undid it' — a repetitive cycle where an action is immediately negated or reversed by another. Common for frustrating situations of never-ending effort: 'as soon as I clean it, it gets dirty again'. Often carries a tone of exasperation.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / V-た + そばから.
formation_notes: Repetitive / futile effort — the main clause undoes the X action.
Common in frustration / exasperation: 教えたそばから忘れる ('as soon as I teach him, he forgets').
Distinct from 〜や否や (single immediate event) and 〜たとたん (immediate follow-up).
Formal / somewhat literary register.

examples.japanese: 教えたそばから忘れてしまう。
examples.english: He forgets as soon as I teach him.

examples.japanese: 片付けたそばから、子供が散らかす。
examples.english: As soon as I clean up, the children make a mess.

examples.japanese: 注意するそばから、また同じ間違いをする。
examples.english: As soon as I warn him, he makes the same mistake again.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / plain form + じゃあるまいし
grammar_points.meaning_en: it's not as if / since it's not like
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜じゃあるまいし expresses 'it's not as if X, so (you should act accordingly)' — a construction that rejects a childish or unreasonable premise. Often used to correct or scold: 'you're not a child, so...' The more formal written form is 〜ではあるまいし.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + じゃあるまいし / ではあるまいし; plain form + わけじゃあるまいし.
formation_notes: Rejects an unreasonable premise — often used to correct or scold.
Common fixed type: 子供じゃあるまいし ('you're not a child, are you?').
The more formal form 〜ではあるまいし is used in writing.
Often paired with a main clause that draws a contrasting appropriate conclusion.

examples.japanese: 子供じゃあるまいし、そんなことで泣かないで。
examples.english: You're not a child — don't cry over that.

examples.japanese: 初めてじゃあるまいし、もう慣れたでしょう。
examples.english: It's not your first time — surely you're used to it by now.

examples.japanese: プロじゃあるまいし、そんなに完璧を求めるな。
examples.english: You're not a pro, so don't demand such perfection.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form / phrase + とばかりに
grammar_points.meaning_en: as if to say / seemingly saying
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とばかりに expresses that someone's action or behavior seems to convey a specific message, without them actually saying it — 'as if to say X'. Common in describing body language, facial expressions, or strong non-verbal signals. Formal / literary register.
grammar_points.formation: Quoted phrase / plain-form clause + とばかりに.
formation_notes: Describes non-verbal communication — actions that convey a message.
Often with strong gestures or looks: 怒ったとばかりに, 嬉しいとばかりに.
Formal / literary register — common in narratives.
Distinct from 〜ばかり (only) and 〜たばかり (just did).

examples.japanese: 彼は「もう限界だ」とばかりに、ため息をついた。
examples.english: He sighed as if to say 'I've had enough'.

examples.japanese: 子供は「早く行こう」とばかりに、私の手を引っ張った。
examples.english: The child pulled my hand as if to say 'let's go quickly'.

examples.japanese: 彼女は「知らない」とばかりに、顔を背けた。
examples.english: She turned her face away as if to say 'I don't know'.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / plain form + とあれば
grammar_points.meaning_en: if it's (a case of) / if it's for
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とあれば expresses a hypothetical or conditional framing — 'if it is the case of X' / 'if it's for X'. Often carries a sense of willingness to act for a specific cause: 'if it's for my family, I'd do anything'. Formal / literary register, common in formal declarations of commitment.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + とあれば; plain-form clause + とあれば.
formation_notes: Formal / literary hypothetical.
Often paired with a willing commitment: あなたのためとあれば、何でもする ('if it's for you, I'd do anything').
Distinct from 〜となれば (if it comes to — more circumstantial).
Formal / somewhat dramatic register.

examples.japanese: あなたのためとあれば、何でもする。
examples.english: If it's for you, I'll do anything.

examples.japanese: 緊急とあれば、すぐ駆けつけます。
examples.english: If it's an emergency, I'll rush over immediately.

examples.japanese: 必要とあれば、お手伝いします。
examples.english: If it's necessary, I'll help.

grammar_points.meaning_en: even if not quite X / not necessarily X, but at least
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ないまでも expresses 'even if not quite X, at least Y' — the speaker concedes that the full X is not achieved, but Y, a lesser but still significant level, is reached. Common for qualifying statements: 'it's not perfect, but it's acceptable'.
grammar_points.formation: V ない + までも.
formation_notes: Hedging concession — falling short of X but reaching Y.
Often paired with せめて ('at least') in the main clause.
Common in softened claims and realistic assessments.
Formal / literary register.

examples.japanese: 完璧とは言わないまでも、かなりよくできている。
examples.english: I wouldn't call it perfect, but it's done quite well.

examples.japanese: 毎日とは言わないまでも、週に一度は運動したい。
examples.english: Not every day, but at least once a week I'd like to exercise.

examples.japanese: 褒めるまでもないが、上手にできた。
examples.english: It's not praise-worthy, but it was well done.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + のもとで / のもとに
grammar_points.meaning_en: under (the guidance / condition / auspices of)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜のもとで / 〜のもとに expresses that an action takes place under the supervision, guidance, conditions, or influence of X — 'under X'. Common with authority figures (under a master, under a teacher), conditions (under certain circumstances), and principles (under a system). Formal register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + のもとで (adverbial) / のもとに (literary variant).
formation_notes: Used for supervision, guidance, conditions, systems.
のもとで is the more common modern form; のもとに is slightly more literary.
Common collocations: 先生のもとで ('under the teacher'), 太陽のもとで ('under the sun'), 厳しい条件のもとで ('under strict conditions').
Distinct from physical 下 ('below / underneath').

examples.japanese: 有名な先生のもとで、ピアノを学んだ。
examples.english: I studied piano under a famous teacher.

examples.japanese: 青空のもとで、ピクニックを楽しんだ。
examples.english: We enjoyed a picnic under the blue sky.

examples.japanese: 厳しい条件のもとに、契約が結ばれた。
examples.english: The contract was concluded under strict conditions.

grammar_points.meaning_en: worth / sufficient to / deserving of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に足る expresses that something is worth doing or merits X — 'worth / sufficient to / deserving of'. More literary than 〜に値する ('worthy of', also N2). Often used in formal evaluations: '信頼に足る' ('worthy of trust'). The attributive 〜に足る + noun is the most common usage.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + に足る; noun + に足る. Attributive: 〜に足る + noun.
formation_notes: Formal / literary 'worthy of'.
Common fixed phrases: 信頼に足る ('worthy of trust'), 尊敬に足る ('worthy of respect').
Less common in speech, more common in written Japanese and formal evaluations.
Similar meaning: 〜に値する (also N2 / slightly different register).

examples.japanese: 彼は信頼に足る人物だ。
examples.english: He is a person worthy of trust.

examples.japanese: これは一読に足る本だ。
examples.english: This is a book worth reading.

examples.japanese: 驚くに足る話ではない。
examples.english: It's not a story worth being surprised about.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb dict / noun + に堪える
grammar_points.meaning_en: can bear / is worth (X-ing)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に堪える expresses that something is tolerable or worth X — 'can bear X / is worth X'. Often used with verbs of evaluation, viewing, or listening: 見るに堪える ('worth watching / bearable to watch'). The negated form 〜に堪えない (see separate entry) is more common and means 'unbearable'.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + に堪える; noun (endurance / evaluation) + に堪える.
formation_notes: Tolerance / worth — 'is worth bearing'.
More common in negated form 〜に堪えない (see separate entry).
Common with 見る, 聞く, 読む: 見るに堪える, 聞くに堪える.
Distinct from 堪える ('endure') in ordinary use — this is the grammatical auxiliary form.

examples.japanese: この作品は鑑賞に堪える。
examples.english: This work is worth appreciating.

examples.japanese: 彼の演奏は聞くに堪える。
examples.english: His performance is worth listening to.

examples.japanese: 子供の絵だが、見るに堪える出来栄えだ。
examples.english: It's a child's drawing, but worth viewing.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb dict / noun + に堪えない
grammar_points.meaning_en: unbearable / can't bear to / not worth
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に堪えない is the negation of 〜に堪える — 'unbearable to X' or 'not worth X-ing'. Common with evaluation verbs: 見るに堪えない ('unbearable to watch'), 聞くに堪えない ('unbearable to listen to'). Also used for emotional states: 感慨に堪えない ('overcome with emotion').
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + に堪えない; noun (emotion) + に堪えない.
formation_notes: Negated form of 〜に堪える — unbearability or 'not worth'.
Common with evaluation: 見るに堪えない, 聞くに堪えない.
Emotional use: 感慨に堪えない ('unable to bear the deep emotion — overcome').
Formal / literary register.

examples.japanese: その映画はひどすぎて、見るに堪えなかった。
examples.english: The movie was so bad it was unbearable to watch.

examples.japanese: 彼の話は聞くに堪えない。
examples.english: His talk is unbearable to listen to.

examples.japanese: 久しぶりの再会に、感慨に堪えなかった。
examples.english: At the long-awaited reunion, I was overcome with emotion.

grammar_points.meaning_en: never stop / can't help (feeling / doing)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜てやまない expresses that a feeling or action never stops — typically used with emotional or volitional verbs to describe ongoing, deeply held sentiments: 'I will never stop hoping', 'I cannot help but admire'. Literary / emphatic register. Common with 願う (wish), 愛する (love), 尊敬する (respect).
grammar_points.formation: V-て + やまない. Usually with emotional / volitional verbs.
formation_notes: Literary / emphatic — for deeply held ongoing feelings.
Common with: 願う, 愛する, 尊敬する, 期待する, 祈る.
やまない comes from 止む (to stop) — 'not stopping'.
Often in dedications, commemorations, formal expressions of sentiment.

examples.japanese: ご成功を祈ってやみません。
examples.english: I pray endlessly for your success.

examples.japanese: 彼の活躍を期待してやまない。
examples.english: I have unending expectations for his success.

examples.japanese: 故郷を愛してやまない。
examples.english: I will never stop loving my hometown.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb ます-stem + そうにない / そうもない
grammar_points.meaning_en: doesn't look like / there's no sign of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜そうにない / 〜そうもない is the negative of the appearance-based そうだ — 'doesn't look like X will happen'. Common with future predictions: the speaker sees no evidence that X will occur. More emphatic than ないだろう. Distinct from the hearsay negative construction.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + そうにない (neutral) / そうもない (slightly more emphatic). Irregular: いい → よさそうにない (rare).
formation_notes: Appearance-based negation — no sign of X happening.
そうもない is slightly more emphatic than そうにない.
Common for predictions: 雨が降りそうにない ('it doesn't look like it'll rain').
Compare with 〜そうだ (positive appearance) — this is its neutral negation.

examples.japanese: 雨が降りそうにない。
examples.english: It doesn't look like it's going to rain.

examples.japanese: 今日は終わりそうもない。
examples.english: It doesn't look like I'll finish today.

examples.japanese: 彼は来そうにないね。
examples.english: He doesn't look like he's coming.

grammar_points.pattern: V negative stem + ずじまい
grammar_points.meaning_en: ended up not (doing) / didn't manage to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ずじまい expresses regret that an intended action was not carried out — 'ended up not X-ing'. Common in retrospectives: 'I meant to do X, but in the end didn't'. Carries a note of disappointment or missed opportunity. Literary / formal register.
grammar_points.formation: V ない-stem + ずじまい (or + で + なる, etc.). Irregular: する → せずじまい.
formation_notes: Retrospective regret — 'didn't end up X-ing, though I meant to'.
Irregular: する → せずじまい (not しずじまい).
Formal / literary register — common in reflective writing.
Related: 〜ずに済む (avoided X, positive nuance) — different from this regret-tinged non-completion.

examples.japanese: 忙しくて、彼に会えずじまいだった。
examples.english: I was so busy I didn't manage to see him.

examples.japanese: 言いたいことも言えずじまいだった。
examples.english: I ended up not saying what I wanted to say.
