examples.japanese: 結局、本を読まずじまいになった。
examples.english: In the end, I didn't manage to read the book.

grammar_points.meaning_en: even if / no matter if (futile effort)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜たところで expresses that even if X is done, the main-clause outcome will not change — 'even if you do X, (Y won't change / matter)'. Often followed by a negative, expressing futility or resignation. Distinct from 〜たところ (N3, aspectual 'just did').
grammar_points.formation: V-た + ところで + (usually negative main clause).
formation_notes: Futility / resignation — the main clause usually expresses 'no matter what, nothing changes'.
Distinct from 〜たところ (N3 aspectual 'just did') and 〜たところが (unexpected contrast).
Often with 〜ない in the main clause.
Formal / literary register.

examples.japanese: 今さら謝ったところで、許してもらえない。
examples.english: Even if you apologize now, you won't be forgiven.

examples.japanese: 走ったところで、もう間に合わない。
examples.english: Even if I run, I won't make it in time.

examples.japanese: 言ったところで、どうにもならない。
examples.english: Even if I say it, nothing will change.

grammar_points.meaning_en: after all / you see / it's because
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: なにしろ is a sentence-initial adverb that introduces an emphatic explanation or justification — 'after all', 'the thing is'. Often used when the speaker wants to stress the reason behind something that the listener might find unusual. Close in function to なにせ (another N2 entry).
grammar_points.formation: なにしろ [sentence]. Often followed by 〜から / のだ / んだ.
formation_notes: Emphatic explanation — 'the reason is, after all'.
Common as a topic-setting adverb when providing context.
Close to なにせ in function; なにしろ is slightly more common.
Often used to explain or justify something unusual.

examples.japanese: なにしろ、彼は経験が豊富だ。
examples.english: After all, he has a wealth of experience.

examples.japanese: なにしろ初めてのことなので、不安だ。
examples.english: You see, it's my first time, so I'm nervous.

examples.japanese: なにしろ忙しかったから、連絡できなかった。
examples.english: The thing is, I was so busy I couldn't contact you.

grammar_points.meaning_en: after all / the thing is
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: なにせ is a sentence-initial adverb very similar in function to なにしろ — introducing an emphatic explanation. 'After all X' or 'The thing is X'. Slightly more casual than なにしろ. Both are used to ground an unusual main-clause statement in a background reason the speaker wants to highlight.
grammar_points.formation: なにせ [sentence]. Often followed by から / のだ / だから.
formation_notes: Near-synonym of なにしろ — slightly more casual in tone.
Signals 'here's the real reason' — emphasizes the following explanation.
Common in conversational Japanese.
Sometimes written 何せ in kanji.

examples.japanese: なにせ、久しぶりだからよく覚えていない。
examples.english: After all, it's been a long time, so I don't remember well.

examples.japanese: なにせ忙しいので、時間がない。
examples.english: The thing is, I'm busy, so I don't have time.

examples.japanese: なにせ初めてなので、ドキドキします。
examples.english: Since it's my first time, I'm nervous.

grammar_points.pattern: V-た form / noun + の + あげく(に)
grammar_points.meaning_en: in the end / after all / finally (negative result)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜あげく expresses that after a long or difficult process, something (usually undesirable) was the final outcome — 'after all the trouble, Y ended up happening'. Carries a negative or disappointing nuance, distinct from 〜末に (after much, often neutral-to-positive). Common in regret / complaint contexts.
grammar_points.formation: V-た + あげく(に); noun + の + あげく(に). Attributive: 〜あげくの + noun.
formation_notes: Negative / disappointing final outcome — after much struggle.
Contrast with 〜末に, which can have a positive or neutral main clause.
Commonly follows drawn-out effort or hesitation.
Formal register — common in stories and formal writing.

examples.japanese: 散々悩んだあげく、結局何も決められなかった。
examples.english: After endlessly worrying, in the end I couldn't decide anything.

examples.japanese: ケンカのあげく、二人は別れてしまった。
examples.english: After the fight, the two ended up breaking up.

examples.japanese: 長い議論のあげく、結論は出なかった。
examples.english: After a long discussion, no conclusion was reached.

grammar_points.meaning_en: on the other hand / while (one side)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜反面 presents two contrasting aspects of the same subject — 'on the one hand X, on the other Y'. Functionally similar to 〜一方で (N3) but with a stronger 'two sides of a coin' feel. Formal / neutral register, common in balanced descriptions of pros and cons.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + 反面; na-adj + な / である + 反面; noun + である + 反面.
formation_notes: 'Two sides of the same thing' — often used for pros/cons or complementary aspects.
Similar to 〜一方で — choice is stylistic.
More formal than a simple が/けど contrast.
Common in essays and balanced assessments.

examples.japanese: この仕事は給料がいい反面、責任が重い。
examples.english: This job has good pay, but on the other hand, heavy responsibility.

examples.japanese: 便利な反面、危険もある。
examples.english: It's convenient, but on the other hand, there's also danger.

examples.japanese: 彼は優しい反面、厳しい面もある。
examples.english: He is kind, yet also has a strict side.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + から見ると / から見れば / から見て
grammar_points.meaning_en: from the viewpoint of / from X's perspective
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜から見ると expresses that an evaluation or observation is made from a specific vantage point — 'from X's viewpoint'. Very close in function to 〜からすると. Common in analytical contexts where the speaker identifies the perspective being adopted.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + から見ると / から見れば / から見て.
formation_notes: Close to 〜からすると — difference is stylistic.
から見て is the adverbial form used when modifying a main clause directly.
Common in analytical / comparative contexts.
Distinct from 〜から見ても ('even from X's view').

examples.japanese: 外国人から見ると、日本は安全な国だ。
examples.english: From a foreigner's perspective, Japan is a safe country.

examples.japanese: 子供から見れば、両親は絶対的な存在だ。
examples.english: From a child's perspective, parents are absolute figures.

examples.japanese: データから見て、この商品は人気がある。
examples.english: Judging from the data, this product is popular.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + ということだ
grammar_points.meaning_en: I hear that / it means that
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ということだ has two related senses: (1) hearsay / report — 'I hear that X' / 'they say X'; (2) meaning / conclusion — 'it means X'. Context disambiguates. Common in news reports and in drawing conclusions from preceding statements. Formal register.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + ということだ / とのことだ (more formal hearsay variant).
formation_notes: Hearsay sense: 'I hear / it is said that'. Common in news.
Meaning sense: 'in other words' — summarizing a preceding explanation.
とのことだ is a more formal hearsay variant, often in business / news.
Compare with 〜そうだ (hearsay, N3) — ということだ is more formal.

examples.japanese: ニュースによると、明日は雨が降るということだ。
examples.english: According to the news, it's going to rain tomorrow.

examples.japanese: つまり、彼は来ないということだ。
examples.english: In other words, it means he isn't coming.

examples.japanese: 田中さんはもう日本を発ったということです。
examples.english: It seems Mr. Tanaka has already left Japan.

grammar_points.pattern: V-た form / noun + の + 結果
grammar_points.meaning_en: as a result of / after
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜結果 (けっか) introduces the result or outcome of a process — 'as a result of X, Y'. Formal / written register, common in reports, essays, and formal narratives. The attributive form 結果の + noun is also productive.
grammar_points.formation: V-た + 結果; noun + の + 結果. Attributive: 〜結果の + noun.
formation_notes: Formal / written — typical of reports, news, and academic writing.
Common with process verbs: 調査した結果, 議論の結果, 話し合いの結果.
Similar in function to 〜あげく (negative) and 〜末に (process).
The noun 結果 literally means 'result / outcome'.

examples.japanese: 調査した結果、新しい事実が分かった。
examples.english: As a result of our investigation, new facts came to light.

examples.japanese: 議論の結果、結論が出た。
examples.english: As a result of the discussion, a conclusion was reached.

examples.japanese: 努力の結果、成功を収めた。
examples.english: As a result of my effort, I achieved success.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + 次第で / 次第では
grammar_points.meaning_en: depending on / according to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜次第で expresses that an outcome or action depends on the X factor — 'depending on X, (the result varies)'. Similar in meaning to 〜によって but with a more variable / contingent feel. The form 〜次第では emphasizes possible variability. Distinct from 〜次第 attached to a verb stem meaning 'as soon as' (see shidai-depending, N3).
grammar_points.formation: Noun + 次第で / 次第では.
formation_notes: Variable outcome depending on X — the main clause outcome varies.
次第で is neutral; 次第では adds emphasis on variability.
Distinct from V ます-stem + 次第 (as soon as) — covered at N3.
Formal register, common in business and policy contexts.

examples.japanese: 天気次第で、予定を変更する。
examples.english: I'll change the plan depending on the weather.

examples.japanese: 結果次第では、再検討も必要だ。
examples.english: Depending on the result, reconsideration may be necessary.

examples.japanese: 君の努力次第で、未来は変わる。
examples.english: Depending on your effort, the future will change.

grammar_points.meaning_en: reach / arrive at / come to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に至る expresses that a process, situation, or movement has reached or extended to X — 'came to X' / 'reached X'. Formal / written register, common in news reports, academic writing, and formal narratives describing development or extent. The attributive form に至る + noun is productive.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に至る; attributive に至る + noun.
formation_notes: Reaching a point / extent — often for processes that culminate in X.
Common in news: 事故に至る経緯 ('the process that led to the accident').
Related: 〜に至るまで (extension to), 〜に至っては (even reaching the point of).
Formal / written register.

examples.japanese: 議論の結果、合意に至った。
examples.english: As a result of the discussion, we reached an agreement.

examples.japanese: この結論に至った経緯を説明します。
examples.english: I will explain how we came to this conclusion.

examples.japanese: 事故に至る原因を調査した。
examples.english: We investigated the causes leading to the accident.

grammar_points.meaning_en: even to / all the way to / as far as
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に至るまで expresses extension or comprehensiveness — 'even reaching X' / 'all the way to X'. Often used to emphasize the thoroughness or extensiveness of something. Common when listing examples that extend into an unexpected or extreme direction. Formal / literary register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に至るまで.
formation_notes: Extension / comprehensiveness — 'even including X'.
Common in exhaustive lists: 髪の毛の色に至るまで ('down even to the color of his hair').
Related: 〜まで (general 'to / until') and 〜に至る (reach).
Formal / written register.

examples.japanese: 子供からお年寄りに至るまで、みんな楽しめる。
examples.english: Everyone, from children all the way to the elderly, can enjoy it.

examples.japanese: 細部に至るまで、丁寧に作られている。
examples.english: It's carefully made all the way down to the details.

examples.japanese: 彼は服装から話し方に至るまで、完璧だ。
examples.english: He's perfect, from his clothing all the way to his way of speaking.

grammar_points.pattern: V plain / noun + の + 際(に)
grammar_points.meaning_en: on the occasion of / at the time of (formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜際(に) marks a significant occasion or moment when something is done — 'on the occasion of X, Y'. More formal than 〜とき and less ceremonial than 〜にあたって. Common in formal announcements, instructions, and business communications: 'in case of emergency, call this number'.
grammar_points.formation: V plain + 際(に); noun + の + 際(に). The に is optional.
formation_notes: Formal temporal marker — for significant moments or occasions.
More formal than 〜とき; less ceremonial than 〜にあたって.
Common in official signage: 非常の際は / ご利用の際には.
Attributive form 〜際の + noun is also productive.

examples.japanese: 非常の際は、このボタンを押してください。
examples.english: In case of emergency, press this button.

examples.japanese: ご利用の際には、注意事項をお読みください。
examples.english: Upon use, please read the precautions.

examples.japanese: 出張の際に、京都に寄った。
examples.english: During my business trip, I stopped by Kyoto.

grammar_points.meaning_en: ahead of / before (others)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に先駆けて expresses being ahead of others or preceding something — 'ahead of X'. Common in business and product release contexts ('ahead of the industry', 'before the competition'). Formal register, often used in marketing and achievement contexts. Distinct from 〜に先立って (before an upcoming event).
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に先駆けて.
formation_notes: Being ahead of / preceding — often with a competitive or innovative nuance.
Common in business: 業界に先駆けて ('ahead of the industry'), 世界に先駆けて ('ahead of the world').
Related: 〜に先立って (before an event); 〜に先駆けて emphasizes being ahead of peers.
Formal / business register.

examples.japanese: 業界に先駆けて、新商品を発売した。
examples.english: Ahead of the industry, we launched the new product.

examples.japanese: 他社に先駆けて、サービスを開始した。
examples.english: Ahead of other companies, we started the service.

examples.japanese: 世界に先駆けて、この技術を開発した。
examples.english: Ahead of the world, we developed this technology.

grammar_points.meaning_en: after going through / via / through (a process)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を経て marks a process or step that was gone through before reaching the main-clause outcome — 'after going through X'. Common in biographies, product development histories, and any narrative involving steps or stages. Formal / written register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を経て.
formation_notes: Going through a process or stage — emphasizes the path taken.
Common in career narratives: 大学を経て, 試験を経て, 経験を経て.
Formal / written — rare in casual speech.
Related: 〜を通じて (through a medium) — を経て is more about sequence.

examples.japanese: 長年の研究を経て、ついに発見した。
examples.english: After many years of research, we finally discovered it.

examples.japanese: 多くの困難を経て、ここまで来た。
examples.english: After going through many hardships, I've come this far.

examples.japanese: 彼は大学を経て、会社員になった。
examples.english: After going through university, he became a company employee.

grammar_points.meaning_en: based on / taking into account / in view of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を踏まえて expresses that the main-clause action or conclusion is based on or takes into account X — 'taking X into account' / 'in light of X'. Formal / business register, common in policy, strategy, and reasoned arguments. The verb 踏まえる literally means 'to step on / base oneself on'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を踏まえて.
formation_notes: Basing / taking into account — grounds the main clause in X.
Common in policy and business contexts: 過去の経験を踏まえて, 現状を踏まえて.
Related: 〜をもとに (based on, slightly less formal), 〜に基づいて (based on).
Formal / business register.

examples.japanese: 現状を踏まえて、対策を立てた。
examples.english: Based on the current situation, we formulated a countermeasure.

examples.japanese: 過去の失敗を踏まえて、計画を見直した。
examples.english: Taking past failures into account, we revised the plan.

examples.japanese: データを踏まえた結論を出した。
examples.english: We came to a conclusion grounded in the data.

grammar_points.meaning_en: ignoring / in disregard of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜をよそに expresses that the main-clause action proceeds in disregard of X — 'ignoring X', 'in spite of X'. Often carries a mild critical nuance, as the X clause typically represents something the subject ought to consider (concerns, warnings, expectations). Formal / literary register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + をよそに.
formation_notes: Disregarding / ignoring — often with a mild critical or disapproving tone.
Common with 心配, 期待, 反対, 注意, 忠告 ('concerns', 'expectations', 'opposition', 'warnings', 'advice').
Formal / literary register — rare in casual speech.
Related: 〜にもかかわらず (despite) — をよそに is more about active disregard.

examples.japanese: 親の心配をよそに、彼は一人で旅に出た。
examples.english: Disregarding his parents' worries, he set off alone on a trip.

examples.japanese: 周囲の反対をよそに、彼らは結婚した。
examples.english: Ignoring the opposition around them, they got married.

examples.japanese: 人々の批判をよそに、彼は自分の道を進んだ。
examples.english: Disregarding people's criticism, he went his own way.

grammar_points.meaning_en: just as / according to / exactly like
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜通り / 〜どおり expresses that an action or outcome is exactly as X was described, predicted, or specified — 'just as X', 'according to X'. The reading shifts between とおり and どおり depending on the preceding word: どおり after nouns (予定どおり), とおり after verb plain forms (言ったとおり).
grammar_points.formation: Noun + どおり; V plain + とおり.
formation_notes: Reading depends on the base: noun + どおり (voiced), verb + とおり (unvoiced).
Common: 予定どおり, 指示どおり, 思ったとおり, 言ったとおり.
Also: 思うとおり ('as one thinks'), 読んで字のごとく ('just as it reads').
Formal / neutral register.

examples.japanese: 予定どおりに出発した。
examples.english: We departed just as planned.

examples.japanese: 先生の指示どおりに行動してください。
examples.english: Please act according to the teacher's instructions.

examples.japanese: 思ったとおりの結果だった。
examples.english: The result was just as I thought.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + いかん / いかんで / いかんによって
grammar_points.meaning_en: depending on / according to (formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜いかん is a formal / literary variant of 〜次第で ('depending on'). Common in business and official statements. Variants: いかんで (depending on), いかんによって (according to), いかんにかかわらず (regardless of). Very formal, rare in casual speech.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + いかん / いかんで / いかんによって / いかんにかかわらず.
formation_notes: Very formal / literary — common in business and official statements.
Related variants: 次第で (more common neutral), いかんで (formal).
The いかんにかかわらず form means 'regardless of'.
Often in notices: 成績いかんで合否が決まる ('pass/fail depends on the grades').

examples.japanese: 結果いかんで、今後の方針を決める。
examples.english: Depending on the result, we'll decide future policy.

examples.japanese: 成績のいかんにかかわらず、全員合格できる。
examples.english: Regardless of grades, everyone can pass.

examples.japanese: 君の頑張りいかんによって、採用が決まる。
examples.english: Depending on your effort, the hiring will be decided.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb ます-stem + 得る / 得ない
grammar_points.meaning_en: is possible / can / is capable of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜得る (うる / える) attaches to a verb's ます-stem to indicate possibility — 'can / is possible to do X'. The dictionary form 得る has two readings: うる (more formal, common in positive) and える (more common in negative 〜えない). Formal / written register. Distinct from the potential form in that 〜得る emphasizes possibility rather than ability.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + 得る (うる / える). Negative: 〜得ない (えない).
formation_notes: Possibility — 'it is possible that X can happen'.
Reading: うる is more formal (especially in positive); えない is the negative.
Common fixed forms: あり得る ('possible'), あり得ない ('impossible').
More formal / literary than the potential form.

examples.japanese: このようなことは起こり得る。
examples.english: Something like this is possible to happen.

examples.japanese: それはあり得ない。
examples.english: That's impossible.

examples.japanese: 考え得る限りの対策を取った。
examples.english: We took every conceivable countermeasure.

grammar_points.meaning_en: because it's / given that it's (special situation)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とあって expresses that the main-clause phenomenon is occurring because of a special circumstance X — 'because it's X (a notable situation), the result is Y'. The X clause is typically a notable or unusual situation, and the main clause describes a resulting atmosphere or behavior. Formal / written register.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause / noun + とあって.
formation_notes: Causal — the X situation is unusual / notable and explains the main clause.
Main clause often describes crowds, atmosphere, or unusual behavior.
Common in news: 休日とあって、公園は大勢の人で賑わった ('because it's a holiday, the park was crowded').
Formal / written register.

examples.japanese: 休日とあって、公園は大勢の人で賑わった。
examples.english: Being a holiday, the park was crowded with many people.

examples.japanese: 有名な歌手が来るとあって、会場は満員だった。
examples.english: Because a famous singer was coming, the venue was full.

examples.japanese: 初めての海外旅行とあって、とても緊張している。
examples.english: Being my first trip abroad, I'm very nervous.

grammar_points.pattern: V-て form + からでないと / からでなければ
grammar_points.meaning_en: not until after / unless you first
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜てからでないと expresses that the main clause cannot happen until after the X action is completed — 'unless you first do X, you can't do Y' / 'not until after X'. The main clause is typically a negative or prohibitive statement. Common in instructions and warnings.
grammar_points.formation: V-て + からでないと / からでなければ + negative main clause.
formation_notes: Prerequisite structure — Y is impossible until X is done.
Main clause must be negative or prohibitive.
Common in instructions: 手を洗ってからでないと、食事できない ('you can't eat unless you first wash your hands').
からでなければ is a slightly more formal variant.

examples.japanese: 説明を聞いてからでないと、始められない。
examples.english: We can't start until after hearing the explanation.

examples.japanese: 手を洗ってからでなければ、食事できません。
examples.english: You cannot eat until you first wash your hands.

examples.japanese: よく考えてからでないと、決められない。
examples.english: I can't decide until after I think it over well.

grammar_points.pattern: V ない-form + ことには
grammar_points.meaning_en: unless / without doing (X, can't do Y)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ないことには expresses that the main-clause action or outcome is impossible unless X is done first — 'unless you do X, (Y is impossible)'. Very close in meaning to 〜てからでないと but structurally attached to the negative form. The main clause is typically negative or expresses inability.
grammar_points.formation: V ない + ことには + negative / inability main clause.
formation_notes: Unless — stronger conditional tied to necessity.
Main clause must be negative or expressing inability.
Structurally related to こと-nominalization.
Formal / neutral register.

examples.japanese: 実際に見てみないことには、分からない。
examples.english: Unless I actually see it, I can't tell.

examples.japanese: お金がないことには、生活ができない。
examples.english: Without money, I can't live.

examples.japanese: 試してみないことには、効果は分からない。
examples.english: Unless you try it, you won't know the effect.

grammar_points.meaning_en: it seems that / appears to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とみえる expresses that something appears to be the case based on observable evidence — 'it seems that X'. Similar to 〜ようだ but with a more observational / neutral feel. Formal / literary register. The と is the quotation/thought particle.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + とみえる (polite: とみえます).
formation_notes: Observational inference — based on evidence the speaker can see.
Close in meaning to 〜ようだ, 〜らしい, 〜みたい — as another evidential.
Formal / literary register.
Can be written 見える or みえる.

examples.japanese: 彼は忙しいとみえる。
examples.english: It seems he is busy.

examples.japanese: 雨が降っていたとみえて、道がぬれている。
examples.english: It seems it was raining — the road is wet.

examples.japanese: 彼は納得したとみえる。
examples.english: He seems to have been convinced.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + とすれば / とすると / としたら
grammar_points.meaning_en: supposing / assuming / if it's the case that
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とすれば (with variants 〜とすると, 〜としたら) expresses a hypothetical assumption — 'supposing that X, then Y'. The speaker accepts X as a premise for the sake of argument and draws a consequence. Formal / neutral register, common in analytical discussions and planning.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause / noun (+ だ) + とすれば / とすると / としたら.
formation_notes: Hypothetical assumption — 'assuming X, then Y'.
Three variants: とすれば (slightly more formal), とすると (neutral), としたら (slightly more conversational).
Common in analytical contexts and planning.
Distinct from 〜たら (simple conditional) in that とすれば emphasizes the assumption.

examples.japanese: もし雨が降るとすれば、どうしますか。
examples.english: Supposing it rains, what will you do?

examples.japanese: 彼の話が本当だとすると、大変なことだ。
examples.english: If his story is true, that's a big problem.

examples.japanese: 宝くじが当たったとしたら、何を買いますか。
examples.english: Supposing you won the lottery, what would you buy?

grammar_points.meaning_en: let alone / not to mention
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜はおろか expresses that even a basic/less-demanding case X is impossible, let alone a more-demanding Y — 'not even X, let alone Y'. The main clause typically presents a more extreme case that is also impossible or absent. Often paired with も, さえ in the main clause for emphasis.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + はおろか + (typically negative / more extreme statement).
formation_notes: 'Not even X, let alone Y' — Y is more demanding than X.
Main clause often has も / さえ / すら for added emphasis.
Literary / formal register.
Compare with 〜どころか (far from) — はおろか more explicitly lists a less-demanding case.

examples.japanese: 漢字はおろか、ひらがなも書けない。
examples.english: Not to mention kanji, I can't even write hiragana.

examples.japanese: 海外旅行はおろか、国内旅行さえしたことがない。
examples.english: Let alone overseas trips, I haven't even done domestic travel.

examples.japanese: 会話はおろか、挨拶もできない。
examples.english: Not to mention conversation, I can't even greet people.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / plain form + にすぎない
grammar_points.meaning_en: is nothing more than / merely
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にすぎない expresses that something is 'nothing more than X' — a dismissive or minimizing description. Common for downplaying the significance of something: 'it's just an excuse', 'it's merely a rumor'. The verb すぎる means 'exceed / go beyond'; the negative form 'not exceed' means 'is no more than'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にすぎない; plain-form clause + にすぎない.
formation_notes: Dismissive / minimizing — 'nothing more than X'.
Common with 言い訳 ('excuse'), 噂 ('rumor'), 推測 ('guess'), 一例 ('one example').
Literally 'does not exceed X' — the すぎない is from the verb 過ぎる.
Formal register, common in essays and arguments.

examples.japanese: それは単なる言い訳にすぎない。
examples.english: That's nothing more than a mere excuse.

examples.japanese: これはほんの一例にすぎない。
examples.english: This is merely one example.

examples.japanese: 彼の話は推測にすぎない。
examples.english: His story is nothing more than speculation.

grammar_points.meaning_en: even if (casual)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜たって / 〜っ たって is a casual contraction of 〜ても ('even if'). Common in casual speech, especially when dismissing a hypothetical or insisting on something despite opposition. Can attach to verbs, adjectives, and nouns. The っ adds emphasis.
grammar_points.formation: V-た (short form) + って; i-adj stem + くたって; na-adj / noun + だって.
formation_notes: Casual contraction of ても — conversational register.
Often in complaints or dismissive reactions: 言ったって分からない ('even if I told you, you wouldn't get it').
For nouns and na-adj: the だって form is very common.
Strictly casual — avoid in formal writing.

examples.japanese: 言ったって、聞いてくれないよ。
examples.english: Even if I told him, he wouldn't listen.

examples.japanese: 高くたって、買いたい。
examples.english: Even if it's expensive, I want to buy it.

examples.japanese: 子供だって分かるよ。
examples.english: Even a kid can understand that.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form / noun + の + こととて
grammar_points.meaning_en: since / because (formal, explanatory)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜こととて is a formal / literary reason marker — 'since X is the case, Y (please excuse)'. Often used in apologies or explanations to acknowledge that circumstances caused an imperfection: 'since I'm new, please forgive any mistakes'. Very formal, rarely used in casual speech.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + こととて; na-adj + な + こととて; noun + の + こととて.
formation_notes: Formal / literary causal — apologetic register.
Very common in polite apologies: 初めてのこととて, 急なこととて.
Similar to から / ので but with an apologetic / formal flavor.
Rare in modern casual speech; common in formal writing.

examples.japanese: 初めてのこととて、不手際をお許しください。
examples.english: Since it's my first time, please forgive any mishandling.

examples.japanese: 急なこととて、ご迷惑をおかけしました。
examples.english: Since it was sudden, I caused you inconvenience.

examples.japanese: 若いこととて、無礼をお許しください。
examples.english: Since he is young, please forgive his rudeness.

grammar_points.meaning_en: as soon as / right after (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: V dict + なり is a literary construction meaning 'as soon as X, Y'. The main clause happens immediately following X, usually unexpectedly or dramatically. Distinct from the other senses of なり ('or' / 'any X or Y'). Formal / literary register.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + なり.
formation_notes: Literary 'as soon as' — similar to や否や and たとたん.
Distinct from other なり senses (listing 'or', indefinite 'any').
Main clause often dramatic or unexpected.
Formal / literary — rare in casual speech.

examples.japanese: 彼は部屋に入るなり、泣き出した。
examples.english: As soon as he entered the room, he burst into tears.

examples.japanese: 彼女は私を見るなり、逃げ出した。
examples.english: As soon as she saw me, she ran away.

examples.japanese: 家に着くなり、すぐ寝てしまった。
examples.english: As soon as I got home, I fell right asleep.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + なりに / なりの
grammar_points.meaning_en: in one's own way / in the manner suited to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜なりに expresses that the main-clause action is done 'in one's own way' or 'according to one's own level / capability' — often with an acknowledging nuance that someone is doing their best within their own limits. Common for modest self-assessments and acknowledgment of effort.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + なりに (adverbial) / なりの + noun (attributive).
formation_notes: Acknowledges personal effort within one's own limits / style.
Common for modest self-assessment: 私なりに頑張った ('I did my best in my own way').
Also for evaluating others: 子供には子供なりの悩みがある ('children have their own kind of worries').
Neutral / slightly formal register.

examples.japanese: 私なりに一生懸命頑張った。
examples.english: I tried my best in my own way.

examples.japanese: 子供には子供なりの悩みがある。
examples.english: Children have their own kind of worries.

examples.japanese: 初心者なりに、工夫してみた。
examples.english: As a beginner, I tried to be creative in my own way.

grammar_points.meaning_en: only possible thanks to / made possible by
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜あっての expresses that the main-clause noun is only possible because of X — 'only possible thanks to X'. The X is presented as essential or indispensable for the existence of the main-clause entity. Formal / literary register, common in heartfelt acknowledgments.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + あっての + noun.
formation_notes: Essential prerequisite — Y exists only because of X.
Common in expressions of gratitude: お客様あっての商売 ('a business that exists only thanks to its customers').
Formal / literary register.
Both nouns in the construction are grammatically tied.

examples.japanese: お客様あっての商売です。
examples.english: It's a business that exists only thanks to our customers.

examples.japanese: 健康あっての人生だ。
examples.english: Life is only meaningful with health.

examples.japanese: 家族あっての幸せだ。
examples.english: Happiness is only possible thanks to family.

grammar_points.meaning_en: as soon as / the moment that (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜が早いか is a literary construction expressing that two events happen in near-immediate succession — 'as soon as / the moment X happens, Y'. Very similar in function to 〜や否や and 〜なり (literary 'as soon as'). Formal / literary register, rare in casual speech.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + が早いか.
formation_notes: Literary 'as soon as' — similar to や否や, なり, か〜ないかのうちに.
Very formal / literary register — common in narratives and dramatic prose.
Main clause event follows immediately and often dramatically.
Rare in modern casual speech.

examples.japanese: ベルが鳴るが早いか、生徒たちは走り出した。
examples.english: The moment the bell rang, the students ran out.

examples.japanese: 彼は座るが早いか、居眠りを始めた。
examples.english: As soon as he sat down, he began to doze off.

examples.japanese: ドアを開けるが早いか、猫が飛び出した。
examples.english: The moment I opened the door, the cat jumped out.

grammar_points.meaning_en: in light of / in accordance with
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に照らして expresses that something is judged or evaluated 'in light of' or 'in accordance with' a standard, rule, or precedent X. Common in legal, policy, and formal analytical contexts — judging an action against a standard. Formal register. The verb 照らす means 'to illuminate / shine on'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun (rule / standard) + に照らして.
formation_notes: Evaluative standard — judging against rules, laws, precedents.
Common with 法律 ('law'), 規則 ('rules'), 基準 ('standards'), 事実 ('facts').
Formal / legal / policy register.
Related: 〜に基づいて (based on) — に照らして more specifically for judgment against standards.

examples.japanese: 法律に照らして、この行為は違法だ。
examples.english: In light of the law, this act is illegal.

examples.japanese: 経験に照らして判断する。
examples.english: I'll judge based on my experience.

examples.japanese: 事実に照らして考えてみよう。
examples.english: Let's think about it in light of the facts.

grammar_points.meaning_en: in view of / considering
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜に鑑みて expresses 'in view of X' or 'considering X' — taking a situation or circumstance into account when making a decision. Very formal, common in legal, governmental, and business documents. The kanji 鑑 means 'reflect / consider'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + に鑑みて.
formation_notes: Very formal 'in view of / considering'.
Common in government and business: 現状に鑑みて, 状況に鑑みて.
Higher register than 〜を踏まえて.
Rare in casual speech — strictly formal writing and speeches.

examples.japanese: 現状に鑑みて、計画を見直す必要がある。
examples.english: In view of the current situation, we need to review the plan.

examples.japanese: 社会情勢に鑑みて、新しい政策を導入した。
examples.english: In view of the social situation, we introduced a new policy.

examples.japanese: 過去の経験に鑑みて、慎重に行動すべきだ。
examples.english: Considering past experience, we should act carefully.

grammar_points.meaning_en: without regard to / disregarding
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜を顧みず expresses that an action is taken 'without regard to' X — the subject ignores X in order to proceed with the main clause action. Often with self-sacrifice: 'without regard to my own safety, I helped'. Formal / literary register. The verb 顧みる means 'to look back / consider'.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + を顧みず.
formation_notes: Self-sacrificial or single-minded disregard — often for noble purposes.
Common nouns: 危険 ('danger'), 家族 ('family'), 自分 ('self'), 健康 ('health').
Formal / literary register, often in honorifics or heroic narratives.
Related: 〜をよそに (disregard, sometimes critical).

examples.japanese: 危険を顧みず、子供を助けた。
examples.english: Disregarding the danger, he helped the child.

examples.japanese: 家庭を顧みず、仕事に没頭した。
examples.english: Without regard to his family, he threw himself into work.

examples.japanese: 自分の健康を顧みず、働き続けた。
examples.english: Without regard to his own health, he kept working.

grammar_points.meaning_en: feel extremely / the limit of (an emotion)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜限りだ expresses that the speaker feels something to an extreme degree — 'I'm extremely X'. Attached to an emotional adjective, it conveys the speaker's heightened emotional state. Formal / literary register, often in sentiment-heavy expressions: 嬉しい限りだ ('I'm extremely happy').
grammar_points.formation: i-adj + 限りだ; na-adj + な + 限りだ.
formation_notes: Emotional intensifier — 'extremely X'.
Common adjectives: 嬉しい, 寂しい, 悲しい, 情けない, 残念な.
Formal / literary register — common in heartfelt statements.
Distinct from 〜限り (as long as / scope — N3).

examples.japanese: あなたにお会いできて、嬉しい限りです。
examples.english: I'm extremely happy to meet you.

examples.japanese: 彼が来ないのは、寂しい限りだ。
examples.english: Him not coming is extremely lonely.

examples.japanese: 力不足で、情けない限りです。
examples.english: Falling short of expectations, I feel utterly ashamed.

grammar_points.pattern: na-adj root + 極まりない / 極まる
grammar_points.meaning_en: extremely / in the extreme
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜極まりない and 〜極まる both express extreme degree — 'extremely X' — attached to an emotional or evaluative na-adjective. Literary / formal register. Interestingly, both the affirmative (極まる) and negative-form (極まりない) mean the same thing — 'reaches the extreme'. Common in literature and formal / emphatic descriptions.
grammar_points.formation: na-adj root + 極まる or 極まりない. Both forms mean 'extremely'.
formation_notes: Literary intensifier — formal / written register.
Curiously, both positive 極まる and negative 極まりない mean the same thing — 'extreme'.
Common na-adjectives: 失礼, 不愉快, 危険, 残念, 無礼.
Rare in casual speech — common in literature and formal criticism.

examples.japanese: 彼の態度は失礼極まりない。
examples.english: His attitude is extremely rude.

examples.japanese: 危険極まる状況だった。
examples.english: It was an extremely dangerous situation.

examples.japanese: 残念極まりない結果だ。
examples.english: It's an extremely regrettable result.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + ほどのことではない / ほどのものではない
grammar_points.meaning_en: not worth that much / not such a big deal
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ほどのことではない dismisses a situation or action as not deserving a strong reaction — 'it's not that big a deal', 'not worth getting upset about'. Often used to calm down someone who is overreacting, or to express humility about one's own achievements. Neutral / formal register.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / noun + ほどのことではない; noun + ほどのものではない.
formation_notes: Dismissive / minimizing — downplays significance.
Often used in reassurance: 心配するほどのことではない ('nothing to worry about').
Can also express humility: 自慢するほどのものではない ('not worth bragging about').
Neutral / slightly formal register.

examples.japanese: 心配するほどのことではないよ。
examples.english: It's not worth worrying about.

examples.japanese: それは謝るほどのことではない。
examples.english: That's not worth apologizing over.

examples.japanese: 大した作品ではないが、自慢するほどのものではない。
examples.english: It's not a great work — nothing to brag about.

grammar_points.meaning_en: even without / even if not
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ずとも is a formal / literary concessive meaning 'even without doing X' or 'even if not X'. Combines the formal negative ず with the concessive とも. Less common than the equivalent 〜なくても but appears in literature, formal speeches, and fixed expressions. Irregular: する → せずとも.
grammar_points.formation: V ない-stem + ずとも. Irregular: する → せずとも.
formation_notes: Formal / literary equivalent of 〜なくても.
Irregular: する → せずとも.
Common in proverbs and fixed expressions.
Rare in modern casual speech — literary register.

examples.japanese: 言わずとも分かるだろう。
examples.english: You should understand without me saying it.

examples.japanese: 急がずとも、間に合うだろう。
examples.english: Even without hurrying, we'll make it in time.

examples.japanese: 心配せずとも大丈夫だ。
examples.english: You needn't worry — it's fine.

grammar_points.pattern: X だの Y だの
grammar_points.meaning_en: things like X and Y (complaining / disparaging listing)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜だの〜だの is a non-exhaustive listing construction with a complaining or disparaging nuance — 'what with X and Y (and all that nonsense)'. Often used when the speaker is frustrated or dismissive about the listed items. Conversational register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun / plain-form predicate + だの + noun / plain-form predicate + だの.
formation_notes: Complaining / dismissive listing — the speaker is frustrated or exasperated.
Can list nouns, verbs, or adjectives.
More emotionally charged than 〜とか or 〜やら.
Common in complaints: 忙しいだの疲れただの、言い訳ばかり ('busy this, tired that, always excuses').

examples.japanese: 忙しいだの疲れただの、言い訳ばかりだ。
examples.english: 'Busy' this, 'tired' that — nothing but excuses.

examples.japanese: 嫌いだの苦手だのと、文句ばかり言う。
examples.english: 'Hate this', 'not good at that' — he just complains.

examples.japanese: 暑いだの寒いだの、いつも不満ばかりだ。
examples.english: 'Too hot', 'too cold' — always complaining about something.

grammar_points.meaning_en: see as / regard as / judge to be
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜とみる expresses that the speaker considers or judges X to be the case — 'I see it as X' / 'I regard it as X'. Formal / analytical register, common in news reports, police statements, and analytical commentary. The verb is 見る used metaphorically as 'judge / consider'.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause / noun + とみる.
formation_notes: Analytical judgment — 'I regard / consider X to be the case'.
Common in news: 警察は犯人は2人組とみている ('police believe the criminals are a pair').
Formal / analytical register.
Close to 〜と考える but more observational.

examples.japanese: 警察は事故の原因を不注意とみている。
examples.english: The police see the cause of the accident as negligence.

examples.japanese: 専門家は今後の見通しを楽観的とみる。
examples.english: Experts see the future outlook as optimistic.

examples.japanese: 彼は成功するとみて間違いない。
examples.english: He's sure to succeed — there's no doubt about it.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + ようで / ようでいて
grammar_points.meaning_en: seemingly X, but actually / looks X but
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ようで expresses apparent contradiction — 'seemingly X, but actually Y'. The surface appearance X contrasts with the deeper reality Y. Often used for surprising insights or revelations: 'he seems quiet, but he's actually funny'. Formal / literary register.
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ようで / ようでいて; na-adj + な / である + ようで; noun + の / である + ようで.
formation_notes: Apparent contradiction — surface vs. reality.
Common in character descriptions and surprising revelations.
ようでいて is slightly more emphatic.
Related: 〜ようだ (looks like — appearance).

examples.japanese: 彼は強そうでいて、実は優しい。
examples.english: He seems tough, but he's actually kind.

examples.japanese: 簡単なようで、実は難しい。
examples.english: It seems simple, but it's actually difficult.

examples.japanese: 忙しいようで、暇そうだ。
examples.english: He seems busy, but actually he looks free.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb ます-stem / noun + さえ + conditional
grammar_points.meaning_en: if only / as long as (the minimal condition is met)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜さえ〜ば / 〜さえすれば expresses a sufficient (often minimal) condition for the main clause to hold — 'as long as X, Y' / 'if only X'. The X is presented as the only thing needed. More emphatic than ば alone. Common in instructions and reassurances.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + さえすれば; noun + さえ + plain-form verb conditional (e.g., さえあれば, さえなければ).
formation_notes: Sufficient condition — X alone is enough for Y.
Common fixed forms: さえあれば ('if only there's X'), さえすれば ('if only one does X').
More emphatic than simple ば conditional.
Related to 〜さえ (even), which is covered separately.

examples.japanese: お金さえあれば、何でも買える。
examples.english: As long as you have money, you can buy anything.

examples.japanese: 勉強さえすれば、合格できる。
examples.english: If only you study, you can pass.

examples.japanese: 時間さえあれば、手伝えるのに。
examples.english: If only I had time, I could help.

grammar_points.meaning_en: because / but / however (casual defensive)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: だって at the beginning of a sentence is a casual way to give a reason or excuse — 'because', 'but'. Often carries a defensive or justifying tone. Common in responses to criticism or when explaining one's position. Strictly casual. Distinct from the particle-use of だって ('even').
grammar_points.formation: だって [sentence].
formation_notes: Casual / defensive — giving a reason in response to a question or criticism.
Distinct from the particle 〜だって ('even / also') used after nouns.
Often paired with から or もん at the end: だって〜もん.
Strictly casual register.

examples.japanese: だって、嫌なんだもん。
examples.english: But I just don't want to!

examples.japanese: だって、本当のことだよ。
examples.english: Because it's the truth.

examples.japanese: だって、知らなかったんだから仕方ない。
examples.english: But I didn't know — it can't be helped.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + といって / これといって
grammar_points.meaning_en: nothing in particular / nothing notable
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜といって (especially in the fixed form これといって) expresses that there is 'nothing in particular' worth noting — 'no specific X' / 'nothing notable'. Very commonly used to downplay or deny significance, often with negative main clauses. Neutral register, very common in everyday speech.
grammar_points.formation: これといって + negative (no particular); noun + といって + negative.
formation_notes: Most commonly in the fixed phrase これといって + negative ('nothing in particular').
Downplays significance — 'nothing notable / worth mentioning'.
Often used to politely deflect: これといって問題はない.
Neutral / common register.

examples.japanese: これといって問題はありません。
examples.english: There's nothing in particular that's a problem.

examples.japanese: これといって趣味はない。
examples.english: I don't have any particular hobbies.

examples.japanese: これといって理由はない。
examples.english: There's no particular reason.

grammar_points.meaning_en: unacceptable / unbecoming / should not
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜まじき is an archaic / literary construction describing behavior as 'unacceptable / unbecoming / should not be done'. Attached to a dictionary-form verb followed by a role noun: 'behavior unbecoming of a doctor'. Appears in formal criticism, editorials, and moralistic statements. Very rare in casual speech.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + まじき + noun (role / position).
formation_notes: Archaic / literary — critical moral judgment.
Fixed pattern: 〜まじき + 行為 / ふるまい ('act / behavior unbecoming of').
The noun is typically a role: 教師, 医者, 警察官, 大人, 政治家.
Rare in casual speech — common in editorials, formal criticism.

examples.japanese: それは教師にあるまじき行為だ。
examples.english: That's behavior unbecoming of a teacher.

examples.japanese: 医者としてあるまじき発言だ。
examples.english: That's an unacceptable statement from a doctor.

examples.japanese: 公務員にあるまじきふるまいだった。
examples.english: It was behavior unbecoming of a civil servant.

grammar_points.meaning_en: if it looks like / if it appears
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ようなら is a conditional variant that attaches to hypothetical situations the speaker infers from appearance — 'if it looks like X, (then do Y)'. Often used when making contingency plans based on how things are going. Distinct from 〜なら (neutral 'if it's X').
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ようなら; na-adj + な + ようなら; noun + の + ようなら.
formation_notes: Appearance-based conditional — 'if it looks like X'.
Distinct from 〜なら (topic conditional) and 〜たら / ば (general conditional).
Common in planning: 雨が降るようなら、中止しましょう ('if it looks like it'll rain, let's cancel').
The longer form ようならば is slightly more formal.

examples.japanese: 雨が降るようなら、中止しましょう。
examples.english: If it looks like it'll rain, let's cancel.

examples.japanese: 難しいようなら、手伝います。
examples.english: If it looks difficult, I'll help.

examples.japanese: 間に合わないようなら、連絡してください。
examples.english: If it looks like you won't make it in time, please contact me.

grammar_points.meaning_en: if it's like that / at this rate
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ようでは expresses a hypothetical condition with a negative, critical, or warning tone — 'if X is like that, (then something bad will follow)'. The main clause often presents an unfavorable prediction or judgment. Distinct from 〜ようなら (more neutral).
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ようでは; na-adj + な + ようでは; noun + の + ようでは.
formation_notes: Critical / warning conditional — implies an unfavorable outcome.
Main clause often: だめだ, 合格できない, 成功しない.
Common in scolding or warning: そんなことを言うようでは、大人ではない.
Distinct from ようなら (neutral hypothesis).

examples.japanese: そんなに怠けるようでは、合格できないよ。
examples.english: If you're lazy like that, you won't pass.

examples.japanese: 約束を破るようでは、信頼できない。
examples.english: If you break promises like that, I can't trust you.

examples.japanese: こんなミスをするようでは、プロとは言えない。
examples.english: If you make mistakes like this, you can't call yourself a pro.

grammar_points.meaning_en: nothing but / entirely / all X
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ずくめ expresses that something is entirely or exclusively X — 'nothing but X', 'all X'. Often used for qualities, colors, outcomes: 黒ずくめ ('all in black'), いいことずくめ ('nothing but good things'), 異例ずくめ ('full of unprecedented things'). Slightly formal / fixed register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + ずくめ. Limited to certain nouns — mostly colors, qualities, notable outcomes.
formation_notes: Restricted productivity — fixed collocations.
Common: 黒ずくめ, いいことずくめ, 異例ずくめ, 初めてずくめ, 記録ずくめ.
Distinct from 〜だらけ (covered with, negative) — ずくめ is more neutral or positive.
Formal / fixed register — not fully productive.

examples.japanese: 彼女はいつも黒ずくめの服を着ている。
examples.english: She is always dressed all in black.

examples.japanese: 今日はいいことずくめの一日だった。
examples.english: Today was a day full of nothing but good things.

examples.japanese: 異例ずくめの出来事だった。
examples.english: It was an event full of unprecedented things.

grammar_points.meaning_en: smeared / covered with (often messy or unpleasant)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜まみれ expresses that something is physically covered or smeared with X, typically an unpleasant or messy substance — mud, blood, dust, oil, sweat. Similar in meaning to 〜だらけ but more specifically about physical coating or smearing. Always negative or messy. Formal register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun (usually liquid / powder / unpleasant substance) + まみれ.
formation_notes: Physical coating / smearing — always negative or messy.
Common: 泥 ('mud'), 血 ('blood'), ほこり ('dust'), 汗 ('sweat'), 油 ('oil').
Similar to 〜だらけ but specifically about being physically covered.
Formal / literary register.

examples.japanese: 子供は泥まみれになって遊んでいた。
examples.english: The child was playing, covered in mud.

examples.japanese: 彼のシャツは血まみれだった。
examples.english: His shirt was covered with blood.

examples.japanese: 長い間使っていなかった本はほこりまみれだった。
examples.english: The long-unused book was covered with dust.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / i-adj stem / na-adj root + ぶる
grammar_points.meaning_en: pretend to be / put on airs of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ぶる creates a verb meaning 'pretend to be X' or 'put on airs of being X'. Often carries a mildly critical nuance — the speaker implies the subject is performing a role rather than being authentically X. Common: 学者ぶる ('play the scholar'), 上品ぶる ('put on refined airs'), いい子ぶる ('pretend to be a good kid').
grammar_points.formation: Noun / i-adj stem / na-adj root + ぶる (conjugates as godan u-verb).
formation_notes: Mildly critical — implies inauthenticity or performance.
Common: 学者ぶる, 上品ぶる, いい子ぶる, 知ったかぶり.
Related noun form: 〜ぶり ('a show of X'), often in 知ったかぶり ('pretending to know').
Neutral to slightly negative register.

examples.japanese: 彼は学者ぶっているけれど、実はよく知らない。
examples.english: He's pretending to be a scholar, but actually he doesn't know much.

examples.japanese: いい子ぶるのはやめなさい。
examples.english: Stop pretending to be a good kid.

examples.japanese: 彼女はいつも上品ぶっている。
examples.english: She's always putting on refined airs.

grammar_points.meaning_en: have a look / hint / feel of
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜めく is a literary suffix creating a verb that means 'have the look / hint / feel of X'. Common with seasonal nouns (春めく 'have a feel of spring') and qualities (謎めく 'seem mysterious'). Literary / formal register, common in descriptive prose and essays.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + めく (conjugates as godan u-verb).
formation_notes: Literary suffix — creates descriptive verbs.
Common: 春めく, 夏めく, 秋めく, 冬めく (seasonal); 謎めく (mysterious), 皮肉めく (ironic).
Literary / formal register — common in descriptive writing.
Attributive form 〜めいた + noun: 謎めいた話 ('a mysterious story').

examples.japanese: 最近、ようやく春めいてきた。
examples.english: Recently, it's finally starting to feel like spring.

examples.japanese: 謎めいた話を聞いた。
examples.english: I heard a mysterious story.

examples.japanese: その言葉には皮肉めいた響きがあった。
examples.english: The words had an ironic ring to them.

grammar_points.meaning_en: there is something (about it) / is impressively
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ものがある expresses that there is a certain quality or feature worth noting about something — 'there's something X about it'. Often used evaluatively with impressive, moving, or remarkable qualities. Formal / literary register. Different from 〜ものだ (general truth) and 〜ものの (although).
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ものがある; na-adj + な + ものがある.
formation_notes: Evaluative — 'there's something X about it'.
Common with impressive / emotional qualities: 感動的なものがある, 素晴らしいものがある.
Formal / literary register, common in essays and reviews.
Distinct from other ものがある phrases (existence of a thing).

examples.japanese: 彼の演奏には感動的なものがある。
examples.english: There's something moving about his performance.

examples.japanese: この町には独特なものがある。
examples.english: There's something unique about this town.

examples.japanese: 彼の考え方には深いものがある。
examples.english: There's something deep about his way of thinking.

grammar_points.meaning_en: or rather / I mean
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜というか is a discourse marker used when the speaker is searching for a more accurate description or hedging a previous statement — 'or rather', 'I mean'. Common in casual speech when revising or qualifying one's own words. Often appears with multiple hedges: 〜というか〜というか.
grammar_points.formation: X というか Y (Y provides an alternative or more accurate description).
formation_notes: Discourse-level hedging — the speaker revises or refines their own statement.
Often chains: 〜というか、〜というか, 〜というか、むしろ〜.
Common in casual / conversational speech when searching for the right word.
Not used in formal writing in this sense.

examples.japanese: 彼は優しいというか、おとなしいタイプだ。
examples.english: He's kind, or rather, a quiet type.

examples.japanese: 疲れたというか、眠いというか、とにかく休みたい。
examples.english: Tired, or sleepy, or something — anyway I want to rest.

examples.japanese: 好きというか、興味がある。
examples.english: I don't 'like' it exactly — I'm interested.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb dict-form + ともなく / ともなしに
grammar_points.meaning_en: unintentionally / without consciously
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ともなく expresses that an action is performed unintentionally, without conscious focus — 'unintentionally X' / 'without meaning to X'. Often with perception or thought verbs: 見るともなく (without consciously watching), 聞くともなく (without consciously listening). Literary register.
grammar_points.formation: V dict-form + ともなく / ともなしに.
formation_notes: Unconscious / unintentional action — typically with perception verbs.
Common with 見る, 聞く, 考える, 待つ.
ともなしに is a slightly more formal variant.
Literary register — rare in casual speech.

examples.japanese: 見るともなく窓の外を眺めていた。
examples.english: I was gazing out the window without really looking.

examples.japanese: 聞くともなしに、彼らの会話を聞いていた。
examples.english: I was listening to their conversation without meaning to.

examples.japanese: 待つともなく待っていたら、彼が現れた。
examples.english: I wasn't really waiting, but he showed up.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun + からいうと / からいえば / からいって
grammar_points.meaning_en: from X's viewpoint / speaking from X
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜からいうと / 〜からいえば / 〜からいって is a variant of 〜からすると that expresses perspective — 'from X's viewpoint'. Common in analysis and judgment contexts. Very close in meaning to 〜からすると but slightly more discourse-oriented ('speaking from').
grammar_points.formation: Noun + からいうと / からいえば / からいって.
formation_notes: Near-synonym of 〜からすると.
Often in analytical or evaluative statements.
からいって is the more adverbial variant (modifying the main clause directly).
Formal / neutral register.

examples.japanese: 結果からいうと、大成功だった。
examples.english: From the results, it was a big success.

examples.japanese: 経験からいえば、早めに行動した方がいい。
examples.english: From experience, it's better to act early.

examples.japanese: 値段からいって、このレストランは高級だ。
examples.english: Judging from the price, this restaurant is upscale.

grammar_points.pattern: V ない-form + くもない / もない
grammar_points.meaning_en: not that I don't / it's not that X isn't
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜なくもない is a double negative that hedges a positive statement — 'it's not that I don't X' / 'it's not impossible that X'. Similar to 〜ないことはない (N3) but slightly more idiomatic. Often expresses reluctant agreement or a mild admission.
grammar_points.formation: V ない-form + (く)もない; i-adj 〜くない + くもない; na-adj + でなくもない.
formation_notes: Double negative — softens a positive claim.
Similar to 〜ないことはない (N3); 〜なくもない is slightly more conversational.
Often expresses reluctant agreement or mild hedging.
Neutral register.

examples.japanese: 行きたくなくもないが、時間がない。
examples.english: It's not that I don't want to go, but I don't have time.

examples.japanese: 彼の意見もわからなくもない。
examples.english: I can sort of see his point.

examples.japanese: そう考えられなくもない。
examples.english: It's not that I can't think of it that way.

grammar_points.meaning_en: depends on / hinges on
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜にかかっている expresses that something 'depends on' or 'hinges on' X — the outcome is determined by X. Common when emphasizing that a decision, success, or failure is contingent on a specific factor. Formal register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + にかかっている (polite: にかかっています).
formation_notes: Emphasizes dependency — 'it all comes down to X'.
Common with responsibility or decisive factors: 成功はあなたにかかっている.
Derived from 掛かる ('to hang on / depend on').
Formal register.

examples.japanese: 成功はあなたの努力にかかっている。
examples.english: Success depends on your effort.

examples.japanese: このプロジェクトの運命は彼の判断にかかっている。
examples.english: The fate of this project hinges on his judgment.

examples.japanese: すべては明日の天気にかかっている。
examples.english: Everything depends on tomorrow's weather.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / plain form + というのは
grammar_points.meaning_en: what is called / the fact is / the reason is
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜というのは has several related senses: (1) 'what is called X / the thing called X' — introducing a definition or concept; (2) 'the fact is / the reason is' — explaining a reason or clarifying a concept. Very common as a topic marker or explanation starter. Neutral register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + というのは; plain-form clause + というのは.
formation_notes: Definition / explanation marker — 'what X is' or 'the reason is'.
Very common in introductions and explanations.
Casual contraction: っていうのは → ってのは.
Close to 〜とは (more literary) in the definitional sense.

examples.japanese: 友情というのは、お金で買えないものだ。
examples.english: Friendship is something you can't buy with money.

examples.japanese: 遅れたというのは、電車が止まったからだ。
examples.english: The reason I was late is that the train stopped.

examples.japanese: 学ぶというのは、楽しいことだ。
examples.english: Learning is a joyful thing.

grammar_points.pattern: V-た form / noun + の + 果てに
grammar_points.meaning_en: at the very end of / after (extensive process)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜果てに expresses that the main-clause outcome came at the end of a long, often harsh, process — 'after extensive X, finally Y'. Typically carries a sense of hardship or dramatic finality. Similar to 〜末に and 〜あげく but with a stronger 'far end' feel. Literary / formal register.
grammar_points.formation: V-た + 果てに; noun + の + 果てに.
formation_notes: Dramatic / final outcome after extensive effort or struggle.
Often negative or weighty — the end of a hard road.
Compare with 〜末に (after much — neutral / positive) and 〜あげく (negative end).
Literary / formal register.

examples.japanese: 長年の努力の果てに、夢を実現した。
examples.english: After years of effort, he finally realized his dream.

examples.japanese: 彼は放浪の果てに、ここに辿り着いた。
examples.english: After long wandering, he arrived here.

examples.japanese: 激しい議論の果てに、ようやく合意に達した。
examples.english: After fierce debate, we finally reached an agreement.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb ます-stem + そびれる
grammar_points.meaning_en: miss the chance to / fail to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜そびれる expresses that someone missed the opportunity to do something or failed to do it despite intending to — 'miss the chance to X'. Common with things the speaker meant to do but didn't: 言いそびれる ('fail to say'), 聞きそびれる ('fail to ask'), 行きそびれる ('miss the chance to go').
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + そびれる (conjugates as ichidan: そびれます, そびれた).
formation_notes: Missed opportunity — implies the speaker meant to but didn't.
Common with communication and travel verbs: 言う, 聞く, 行く, 会う, 帰る.
Related in feeling to 〜ずじまい (ended up not).
Conversational register.

examples.japanese: 言いそびれたことがある。
examples.english: There's something I missed the chance to say.

examples.japanese: 忙しくて、昼ごはんを食べそびれた。
examples.english: I was so busy I missed my chance to eat lunch.

examples.japanese: 聞きそびれてしまった。
examples.english: I missed the chance to ask.

grammar_points.pattern: V-て form + しかるべき
grammar_points.meaning_en: should be (fittingly) / ought to be
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜てしかるべき expresses that an action or state 'should be' or 'is fitting' — 'ought to be done' with a sense of fairness or propriety. Very formal / literary register, common in formal criticism and arguments about what should be done. The verb しかる is literary for 'to be fitting'.
grammar_points.formation: V-て + しかるべき (+ だ, or as attributive before noun).
formation_notes: Formal / literary 'ought to be'.
Implies fairness or propriety — what 'ought' fittingly to happen.
Common in formal critique: 謝罪があってしかるべきだ ('there ought to be an apology').
Very rare in casual speech.

examples.japanese: 彼には謝罪があってしかるべきだ。
examples.english: There ought to be an apology from him.

examples.japanese: その程度の配慮はあってしかるべきだ。
examples.english: That level of consideration ought to exist.

examples.japanese: 責任者には辞任を求めてしかるべきだ。
examples.english: The person in charge should fittingly be asked to resign.

grammar_points.meaning_en: (emphatic) not at all / definitely not (casual)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜やしない is a casual emphatic negative — 'definitely won't / absolutely not'. Attached to verb negative forms to strengthen the negation. Common in frustrated or emphatic denials. Strictly casual register.
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + やしない (most common) or V ない-form + やしない.
formation_notes: Casual emphatic negation — 'definitely won't'.
Often in frustrated complaints: 分かりやしない, 聞きやしない.
Distinct from 〜っこない (absolute impossibility, similar register).
Strictly casual — avoid in polite speech.

examples.japanese: そんなこと、分かりやしない。
examples.english: There's no way I'd understand that.

examples.japanese: 誰も聞きやしないよ。
examples.english: Nobody's going to listen.

examples.japanese: 彼は気にしやしない。
examples.english: He doesn't care at all.

grammar_points.meaning_en: do X a lot / do X extensively / keep doing
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜まくる is a casual auxiliary meaning 'do X a lot / extensively / to excess'. Attached to verb ます-stems, it emphasizes heavy, continuous, or extensive engagement in an activity. Common in casual / slangy speech: 食べまくる ('eat a lot'), 遊びまくる ('play around a lot'), 書きまくる ('write a lot').
grammar_points.formation: V ます-stem + まくる (conjugates as godan u-verb).
formation_notes: Emphatic extension — 'do a lot of X'.
Casual / slang register — do not use in formal speech.
Common with consumption and action verbs.
Similar in function to 〜抜く (to the end) but more about quantity than endurance.

examples.japanese: 試験前に勉強しまくった。
examples.english: I studied a ton before the exam.

examples.japanese: 休みの日は寝まくる。
examples.english: On my days off, I sleep a lot.

examples.japanese: 彼は一日中ゲームをやりまくっている。
examples.english: He's playing video games all day long.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + ということは
grammar_points.meaning_en: what it means is / so (it follows that)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ということは is a discourse marker that introduces a logical conclusion from a preceding statement — 'what this means is', 'in other words', 'so it follows that'. Very common in spoken Japanese for drawing inferences. Distinct from 〜ということだ (hearsay / 'it means').
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + ということは.
formation_notes: Draws a conclusion / inference from a preceding statement.
Very common in conversation when summarizing or clarifying.
Casual contraction: ってことは.
Distinct from 〜ということだ (the final summary 'it means X').

examples.japanese: 彼が来ないということは、忙しいのだろう。
examples.english: His not coming means he's probably busy.

examples.japanese: この結果が出たということは、実験が成功したということだ。
examples.english: The fact that we got this result means the experiment was a success.

examples.japanese: 明日休みだということは、今夜遅くまで起きていられる。
examples.english: Tomorrow being a holiday means I can stay up late tonight.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form / noun + の + ごとく
grammar_points.meaning_en: just as / like / as if (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ごとく is a literary / classical construction meaning 'just as' or 'like' — used for similes and comparisons in formal and literary writing. Derived from the classical adjective ごとし. Very formal, common in literature, essays, and formal speeches. Attributive form ごとき + noun is also productive.
grammar_points.formation: V plain + (が)ごとく; noun + の + ごとく. Attributive: ごとき + noun.
formation_notes: Literary / classical simile marker — 'just as'.
Very formal / written register.
Attributive form 〜ごとき: 矢のごとき速さ ('speed like an arrow').
Modern equivalent: 〜のように (more common, informal).

examples.japanese: 矢のごとく、時間が過ぎていく。
examples.english: Time passes just like an arrow.

examples.japanese: 彼は狂ったごとく働き続けた。
examples.english: He kept working as if possessed.

examples.japanese: 夢のごとく美しい景色だ。
examples.english: It's a scene beautiful like a dream.

grammar_points.meaning_en: like (a) / such as (literary, often disparaging)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ごとき is the attributive form of ごとし, meaning 'like X' or 'such as X'. Often carries a dismissive or belittling nuance when used with a noun referring to a person or entity — 'someone like X (who is beneath / below expectations)'. Literary / formal, also used self-deprecatingly.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + ごとき (+ noun).
formation_notes: Often dismissive when used with people: 私ごとき ('someone like me'), お前ごとき ('someone like you').
Can also be a neutral simile with other nouns.
Literary / classical register.
Self-deprecating with 私ごとき expresses humility.

examples.japanese: 私ごとき者にはもったいないお言葉です。
examples.english: Such kind words are wasted on someone like me.

examples.japanese: お前ごときに負けはしない。
examples.english: I won't lose to someone like you.

examples.japanese: 子供ごときに負けて悔しい。
examples.english: I'm frustrated to lose to a mere child.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form + かというと / かといえば
grammar_points.meaning_en: as for whether / if you ask whether
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜かというと embeds a question for discussion — 'as for whether X, (the answer is Y)'. Often used when the speaker raises a question and then answers it themselves. Common in explanatory contexts and hedged assertions. Variants: どちらかというと ('if anything / rather').
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + かというと / かといえば. Common fixed phrase: どちらかというと.
formation_notes: Discourse-level 'as for whether' — raising and answering a question.
Common fixed form: どちらかというと ('if anything / rather'), どちらかといえば.
Often with hedged or surprising answers.
Neutral register.

examples.japanese: 好きかというと、そうでもない。
examples.english: As for whether I like it — not really.

examples.japanese: どちらかといえば、犬より猫の方が好きだ。
examples.english: If anything, I prefer cats to dogs.

examples.japanese: 上手かというと、まだまだだ。
examples.english: As for whether I'm good at it — not yet, by far.

grammar_points.meaning_en: as / regarded as / assuming that
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ものとして is used to frame something as being treated or considered as X — 'as X' or 'regarded as X'. Often in hypothetical or categorical framings: 'treat this as accepted', 'consider it settled'. Formal / written register, common in business and formal statements.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + ものとして; plain-form clause + ものとして.
formation_notes: Categorical framing — 'considered as X' for practical purposes.
Common in business and formal contexts.
Also: 〜たものとして ('regard as having been X').
Formal register.

examples.japanese: 本件は合意したものとして扱います。
examples.english: We will treat this matter as agreed upon.

examples.japanese: これは正式な書類として受け取ってください。
examples.english: Please accept this as an official document.

examples.japanese: そう決まったものとして、次に進みましょう。
examples.english: Assuming that's decided, let's move on.

grammar_points.pattern: V-て form + もいいくらい
grammar_points.meaning_en: enough to / to the extent that (you could)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜てもいいくらい expresses that the intensity or extent of something is so high that it justifies an extreme action — 'it's so X that you might as well Y'. Often used hyperbolically. Close to 〜ほど with extreme degree meaning. Neutral / casual register.
grammar_points.formation: V-て + もいいくらい.
formation_notes: Hyperbolic degree — 'enough to do X / to the extent that'.
Often used emphatically: 感謝してもしきれないくらい ('so grateful I can't thank you enough').
Combines 〜てもいい (may) with くらい (extent).
Neutral / casual register.

examples.japanese: 泣いてもいいくらい感動した。
examples.english: I was moved enough that I could have cried.

examples.japanese: 叫んでもいいくらい嬉しかった。
examples.english: I was so happy I could have shouted.

examples.japanese: 踊ってもいいくらいの気分だ。
examples.english: I feel so good I could dance.

grammar_points.meaning_en: the rest is / all that's left is
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜あとは expresses that after a main action is complete, only X remains to be done — 'the rest is X' / 'all that's left is X'. Common in task-oriented contexts for summarizing what's still to be finished after the main work is done.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + あとは; V plain + あとは.
formation_notes: 'Remaining to be done' — describes what's left after the main effort.
Common for project wrap-up or recipe contexts.
Distinct from 〜あとで (after doing) — あとは is about what remains.
Neutral register.

examples.japanese: 準備はできた。あとは出発するだけだ。
examples.english: Preparations are done. All that's left is to depart.

examples.japanese: 書類を作成した。あとは上司の承認を待つだけだ。
examples.english: I prepared the documents. The rest is just waiting for the boss's approval.

examples.japanese: 材料は全部そろった。あとは料理するだけだ。
examples.english: All the ingredients are ready. The rest is just cooking.

grammar_points.meaning_en: although / even though (literary)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜といえども is a formal / literary concessive meaning 'even though X' or 'although X'. Often used when the speaker acknowledges that X is the case but presents a contrasting main clause. More formal than 〜とはいえ. Common in speeches, essays, and literary writing.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause / noun + といえども.
formation_notes: Very formal / literary concessive — rare in casual speech.
More formal than 〜とはいえ or 〜にしても.
Often with contrasts about capability, position, or principle: 子供といえども ('even a child, though...').
Common in speeches and formal prose.

examples.japanese: 子供といえども、約束は守るべきだ。
examples.english: Even a child should keep a promise.

examples.japanese: プロといえども、失敗することはある。
examples.english: Even a professional sometimes fails.

examples.japanese: 王様といえども、ルールには従うべきだ。
examples.english: Even a king should follow the rules.

grammar_points.meaning_en: but contrary to expectation / I thought but
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜と思いきや expresses that the speaker's expectation was contradicted by reality — 'I thought X, but (the reality was Y)'. Literary register. Often used in narratives for surprising reversals of expectation.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form clause + と思いきや.
formation_notes: Surprise reversal — expectation contradicted by reality.
Literary / narrative register.
Main clause typically presents something unexpected.
Common in stories and formal writing.

examples.japanese: 簡単と思いきや、意外に難しかった。
examples.english: I thought it would be easy, but it was surprisingly hard.

examples.japanese: 晴れると思いきや、雨が降ってきた。
examples.english: I thought it would be sunny, but it started raining.

examples.japanese: 勝てると思いきや、逆転負けしてしまった。
examples.english: I thought I'd win, but I ended up losing in an upset.

grammar_points.meaning_en: at least / whatever / even just
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜なりとも expresses 'at least X' or 'even just X' — a concessive minimizer acknowledging a small or minimal offer. Often in polite offers or requests: 少しなりとも ('even just a little'). Formal / literary register. Distinct from 〜なり (as soon as) and 〜なりに (in one's own way).
grammar_points.formation: Noun + なりとも.
formation_notes: Minimizer — 'at least X' or 'even just X'.
Common in polite offers: 少しなりともお役に立ちたい ('I'd like to be of at least some help').
Formal / literary register.
Distinct from other なり constructions.

examples.japanese: 少しなりともお役に立ちたい。
examples.english: I'd like to be of at least some help.

examples.japanese: 一言なりとも、挨拶すべきだった。
examples.english: I should have at least said a word of greeting.

examples.japanese: 多少なりともご協力いたします。
examples.english: I will offer at least some cooperation.

grammar_points.pattern: Noun / V plain + だけなら
grammar_points.meaning_en: if only / if it's just X
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜だけなら expresses 'if it's only X (then Y is OK)' — a conditional statement that limits the scope to a minimal case. Often used when the speaker is willing to accept or permit something up to a specific boundary. Neutral register.
grammar_points.formation: Noun + だけなら; V plain + だけなら.
formation_notes: Minimal-scope conditional — 'if just X, it's fine'.
Common in permission or hedged acceptance.
Related: 〜だけ (only) + なら (conditional).
Neutral register.

examples.japanese: 見るだけなら、いいですよ。
examples.english: If it's just looking, it's fine.

examples.japanese: それだけなら、私でもできる。
examples.english: If it's just that, even I can do it.

examples.japanese: ちょっとだけなら、貸してあげる。
examples.english: If it's just a little, I'll lend it to you.

grammar_points.pattern: V ない + のみならず
grammar_points.meaning_en: not only does not / not just failing to
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: 〜ないのみならず is a literary concessive-additive combining the negative ない with the 'not only' のみならず — 'not only does X not happen, (but Y also)'. The Y clause extends the negation or adds a worse case. Formal / literary register, rare in casual speech.
grammar_points.formation: V ない / noun + でない + のみならず.
formation_notes: Literary / formal extended negation.
Common in essays and formal criticism.
Combines 〜ない (negative) + 〜のみならず ('not only').
Rare in casual speech.

examples.japanese: 彼は謝らないのみならず、逆に怒り出した。
examples.english: Not only did he not apologize, but he even got angry.

examples.japanese: 問題は解決しないのみならず、悪化している。
examples.english: Not only is the problem not solved, but it's getting worse.

examples.japanese: 返事をくれないのみならず、連絡も取れない。
examples.english: Not only does he not reply, I can't even contact him.

grammar_points.pattern: Verb-stem / い-adj stem / な-adj root + そう(だ/です)
grammar_points.meaning_en: looks like / seems (appearance-based judgment)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: そうだ attached to a verb stem, い-adjective stem, or na-adjective root expresses a judgment based on appearance — 'it looks like', 'it seems'. The speaker is making an inference from visual or situational evidence, not reporting something they heard. This is structurally distinct from the hearsay そうだ which attaches to plain form.
grammar_points.formation: Verb ます-stem + そう; い-adj drop い + そう (高い → 高そう); な-adj root + そう (元気 → 元気そう). Exceptions: いい → よさそう, ない → なさそう.
formation_notes: For verbs, use the ます-stem: 食べる → 食べそう, 降る → 降りそう.
Negative of verb appearance: 〜そうにない / 〜そうもない (doesn't look like).
Negative of い-adjective appearance: 〜くなさそう (高くない → 高くなさそう).
Functions as a な-adjective grammatically: おいしそうな料理, 元気そうに見える.

examples.japanese: 雨が降りそうです。
examples.english: It looks like it's going to rain.

examples.japanese: このケーキはおいしそうですね。
examples.english: This cake looks delicious, doesn't it?

examples.japanese: 彼は元気そうに見える。
examples.english: He looks energetic.

grammar_points.meaning_en: I heard that / apparently (reported speech)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: When そうだ attaches to a plain-form predicate (not a verb stem), it expresses hearsay — 'I heard that ___'. The speaker is reporting information received from another source rather than making their own judgment. The source can be named with 〜によると ('according to ___'). Structurally distinct from appearance-based そうだ, which attaches to the verb stem.
grammar_points.formation: Plain-form verb / i-adj / na-adj / noun + だ (for nouns and na-adj) + そうだ. Examples: 食べるそうだ, 食べたそうだ, 高いそうだ, 学生だそうだ, 元気だそうだ.
formation_notes: Note: for nouns and na-adjectives, the だ is retained before そうだ (学生だそうだ, not 学生そうだ — the latter would be an appearance-based construction).
No distinct past form for the hearsay そうだ itself — the tense is inside the reported clause (食べたそうだ = 'I heard they ate').
Cannot be negated as a whole unit — negate the reported content instead (来ないそうだ = 'I heard they're not coming').
The source of the hearsay is commonly introduced with 〜によると or 〜の話では.

examples.japanese: 天気予報によると、明日は雨だそうです。
examples.english: According to the weather forecast, it will rain tomorrow.

examples.japanese: 田中さんは来月結婚するそうです。
examples.english: I heard Mr. Tanaka is getting married next month.

examples.japanese: あの店のラーメンはおいしいそうだ。
examples.english: I hear the ramen at that restaurant is good.

grammar_points.meaning_en: appears / seems (evidence-based, formal)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: ようだ expresses an inference the speaker is making based on available evidence — 'it appears that', 'it seems that'. It is more formal and more tentative than みたい (its casual equivalent). It is also used for similes ('like X') and has a highly productive adverbial form ように ('in a way such that').
grammar_points.formation: V plain / i-adj + ようだ. Na-adj + な + ようだ (静かなようだ). Noun + の + ようだ (学生のようだ). Past: 食べたようだ, 高かったようだ.
formation_notes: Note the different connectors: な for na-adjectives, の for nouns — NOT だ.
Also used for similes: 彼は子供のようだ ('he is like a child').
The adverbial form ように is highly productive — see you-ni-purpose for the purpose construction.
より formal/written than みたい; roughly interchangeable in meaning.

examples.japanese: 誰もいないようだ。
examples.english: It seems nobody is here.

examples.japanese: 彼は疲れているようだった。
examples.english: He appeared to be tired.

examples.japanese: この部屋は事務所のようだ。
examples.english: This room is like an office.

grammar_points.pattern: Plain form / noun + みたい(だ/です)
grammar_points.meaning_en: looks like / seems / is like (casual)
grammar_points.meaning_detailed: みたい is the casual equivalent of ようだ, expressing inference or similarity based on appearance or evidence. Unlike ようだ, it attaches directly to nouns without の, and directly to na-adjectives without な. It can function as a na-adjective itself (みたいな + noun, みたいに adverbially).
grammar_points.formation: Noun + みたい (学生みたい). V plain + みたい (降るみたい). i-adj + みたい (高いみたい). na-adj + みたい (静かみたい — no な). Past: 降ったみたい.
formation_notes: No connector particle for nouns or na-adjectives (unlike ようだ, which uses の and な respectively).
As a na-adjective: みたいな N (attributive), みたいに + adjective/verb (adverbial).
Interchangeable with ようだ in most contexts but more casual; avoid in formal writing.

examples.japanese: 彼は日本人みたいだ。
examples.english: He seems like a Japanese person.

examples.japanese: 雨が降るみたいですよ。
examples.english: It looks like it's going to rain.

examples.japanese: 子供みたいに笑った。
examples.english: I laughed like a child.

grammar_points.meaning_en: seems / apparently / typical of
